title: Apache Airflow for Data Engineers: Senior-Level Track
description: Design a multi-team, multi-country orchestration platform on Airflow — deployment topology, namespace conventions, DAG ownership, environment strategy, and the organizational patterns that prevent a 200-DAG platform from collapsing under its own weight.

chapters.title: Platform Architecture at Scale
chapters.description: Design a multi-team, multi-country orchestration platform on Airflow — deployment topology, namespace conventions, DAG ownership, environment strategy, and the organizational patterns that prevent a 200-DAG platform from collapsing under its own weight.

blocks.content: The mid-level track taught you to operate SaulėGrid's production Airflow platform: data-aware scheduling with Datasets, custom operators for SFTP ingestion and regulatory submission, idempotent pipeline design, CI/CD deployment, RBAC for three teams, scheduler tuning, and operational runbooks. You can take an orchestration requirement and deliver a DAG that runs reliably, recovers from failures, and meets its SLA. That is the skill of a mid-level platform engineer. The senior-level shift is from operating a platform to designing the platform that makes operations possible for everyone. When SaulėGrid had one team, 12 DAGs, and one Airflow instance, any engineer could hold the entire system in their head. When BalticSolar Alliance formed — merging SaulėGrid (Lithuania), LatSolar (Latvia), and EstEnergy (Estonia) into a single energy alliance — that mental model collapsed within the first quarter.
BalticSolar Alliance — The Scenario

BalticSolar Alliance is the post-acquisition entity formed when SaulėGrid acquired LatSolar and EstEnergy. The combined platform serves 4 million smart meters across 30 regions in 3 countries, generating 4 million new rows per day across meter readings, solar inverter telemetry, battery state-of-charge, and weather forecasts. The historical archive exceeds 25TB and grows by 10GB daily. Ten teams contribute to the platform: three country-level data engineering teams (one per subsidiary), one central platform engineering team, three analytics teams (commercial, regulatory, and market), two ML teams (demand forecasting and battery optimization), and one regulatory compliance team that spans all three countries.

Each team operates with partial autonomy — they own their DAGs, their Gold tables, and their deployment schedules — but they share the Airflow infrastructure, the Bronze and Silver layers, and the scheduling resources. The tension between autonomy and shared infrastructure is the central architectural challenge of this module. Every architectural decision balances team independence against platform coherence: too much independence creates silos where each team reinvents conventions; too much central control creates bottlenecks where every DAG change requires platform team approval.
Why the Mid-Level Architecture Breaks

rows: 3 countries with data residency requirements and 200+ DAGs
200 DAGs where names collide and ownership is ambiguous
3 countries with different regulatory schedules and release cadences
Cross-country dependencies where the upstream DAG is on a different Airflow instance
3 instances with country-specific configuration and 10 teams with conflicting preferences
10 teams with no shared conventions and no code review standards
Large fleet, 3 countries, 10 teams with unequal usage

blocks.content: Every row in that table represents a real incident from BalticSolar's first quarter. The single-instance architecture hit its scheduling ceiling when the combined DAG count exceeded 150 and parsing delay pushed scheduling latency past the regulatory SLA. The flat naming convention produced a collision when both the Lithuanian and Estonian teams deployed a DAG called dag_bronze_meters on the same day. The shared staging environment broke when a Latvian config change disrupted Lithuanian DAG testing. These are not theoretical risks — they are the operational reality of scaling from a single-team platform to a multi-country alliance.
The Scale Numbers
What This Module Covers

This module addresses the organizational architecture of a multi-team orchestration platform. The lessons are not primarily about Airflow Python code — they are about the structures, contracts, and processes that make Airflow manageable at scale. Topology design determines how Airflow instances are deployed and coordinated. Namespace conventions determine how DAGs are named, discovered, and owned. Environment topology determines how changes flow from development to production. Cross-country coordination determines how DAGs on separate instances depend on each other. Configuration architecture determines how settings are managed and promoted. Repository structure determines how DAG code is organized and reviewed. Cost allocation determines how infrastructure spending is attributed. Observability determines how the platform's health is monitored across instances.

The most dangerous number in the scale table is not the largest multiplier — it is the DAG count. Going from 12 to 200+ DAGs is not a linear scaling problem. At 12 DAGs, a single engineer can review every DAG's code, know every schedule, and diagnose any failure from memory. At 200+ DAGs, that is impossible. The platform must be self-documenting (namespaces reveal ownership and purpose), self-governing (CI enforces conventions without human review of every DAG), and self-monitoring (alerts detect problems that no single engineer is watching for). Every architectural decision in this module is evaluated by this criterion: does it make the platform manageable without requiring any single person to hold the entire system in their head?

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The purpose of platform architecture is to make the default path the correct path. If the default namespace convention prevents naming collisions, engineers do not need to coordinate names. If the default ownership model prevents unauthorized DAG modifications, engineers do not need to negotiate access. If the default CI pipeline validates DAG conventions, engineers do not need to manually review every deployment. Every architectural decision in this module is evaluated by whether it makes correct behavior automatic and incorrect behavior difficult.

The first architectural decision for any multi-country Airflow platform is topology: how many Airflow instances, where they run, and how they coordinate. This decision constrains every subsequent choice — namespace design, environment strategy, scheduling governance, and observability architecture all depend on whether the platform operates as one shared instance or multiple federated instances. The mid-level track assumed a single Airflow instance because SaulėGrid operated in one country with one regulatory regime. BalticSolar Alliance cannot make that assumption. Lithuanian energy data is subject to VERT regulations, Latvian data to SPRK, and Estonian data to Konkrentsiiamet. Each regulator has different data residency requirements, audit retention periods, and reporting deadlines.
Three Topology Models

There are three viable models for multi-country orchestration, and each optimizes for a different constraint. The choice is not about which model is best in the abstract — it is about which constraint the organization cannot violate. An organization whose primary constraint is regulatory isolation will choose differently from one whose primary constraint is operational cost. Understanding the three models and their trade-off profiles is the foundation for every topology decision in this module.
Model 1: Shared Instance

A single Airflow instance serves all three countries. All DAGs run on the same scheduler, share the same metadata database, and execute on the same worker fleet. Country isolation is achieved through RBAC (each team sees only its DAGs) and connection scoping (each team accesses only its country's databases). This model minimizes operational overhead — one instance to monitor, one scheduler to tune, one metadata database to maintain. But it concentrates risk: a scheduler failure affects all three countries, a metadata database corruption affects every DAG, and a worker fleet capacity issue creates cross-country resource contention. Most critically, RBAC provides logical isolation, not physical isolation. A misconfigured DAG could theoretically access another country's connections.
Model 2: Federated Instances

Each country operates its own Airflow instance with its own scheduler, metadata database, worker fleet, and deployment pipeline. Country isolation is physical: Lithuanian data never touches Latvian infrastructure. Each instance can be configured independently — different executor types, different Airflow versions during staged upgrades, different scheduler tuning. The blast radius of any failure is limited to one country. But the operational overhead triples: three schedulers to monitor, three metadata databases to maintain, three deployment pipelines to manage. Cross-country DAG dependencies require an external coordination mechanism because Datasets and ExternalTaskSensors work only within a single instance.
Model 3: Hybrid Topology

A central coordinator instance manages cross-country orchestration — alliance-wide Gold aggregation DAGs, cross-border data exchange DAGs, and the scheduling governance dashboard — while per-country instances handle country-specific workloads with physical isolation. The coordinator does not process country data directly; it triggers and monitors cross-country workflows by communicating with country instances through the REST API. This model provides physical isolation for country data, limited blast radius per country, and centralized cross-country coordination. The trade-off is complexity: the coordinator adds a fourth instance to manage, the cross-instance communication introduces latency and failure modes, and the split between coordinator-owned and country-owned DAGs requires clear governance.

left.label: Fewer Instances (Shared)
points: Lower operational cost (one scheduler, one DB, one deployment). Simpler cross-DAG dependencies (everything on one instance). Lower infrastructure spend. But: shared blast radius, logical-only isolation, single point of failure for scheduling, RBAC as the sole isolation boundary.

right.label: More Instances (Federated/Hybrid)
points: Physical regulatory isolation. Limited blast radius per country. Independent upgrade cycles. Country-specific executor selection. But: higher operational overhead (3–4 instances), cross-instance coordination complexity, configuration drift risk, higher infrastructure cost.

blocks.content: BalticSolar's Topology Decision

blocks.title: BalticSolar Alliance Airflow topology
blocks.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   COORDINATOR INSTANCE (EU-CENTRAL)     │
│   • Alliance-wide Gold DAGs             │
│   • Cross-border exchange DAGs          │
│   • Scheduling governance dashboard     │
│   • Cross-instance Dataset registry     │
└──────┬──────────────┬──────────────┬─────┘
   REST│API       REST│API       REST│API
┌──────┴──────┐ ┌─────┴───────┐ ┌────┴────────┐
│ LT INSTANCE │ │ LV INSTANCE │ │ EE INSTANCE │
│ CeleryExec  │ │ Hybrid Exec │ │ K8s Exec    │
│ 12 regions  │ │ 10 regions  │ │ 8 regions   │
│ 80 DAGs     │ │ 50 DAGs     │ │ 40 DAGs     │
│ VERT regs   │ │ SPRK regs   │ │ Konkurentsi │
└─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘
blocks.caption: BalticSolar uses a hybrid topology: a central coordinator handles alliance-wide orchestration while per-country instances handle country-specific workloads with physical isolation. Each country uses a different executor based on workload characteristics.

visual-recommendation.description: Architecture diagram showing the four-instance Airflow topology with coordinator connecting to three country instances via REST API, each with different executors, DAG counts, and regulatory labels. Include network boundaries.

blocks.content: BalticSolar chose the hybrid model because the regulatory constraint was non-negotiable: VERT requires that Lithuanian meter data processing infrastructure be hosted within Lithuanian jurisdiction, and the same applies to SPRK for Latvia and Konkrentsiiamet for Estonia. A single shared instance could not satisfy all three residency requirements simultaneously. The fully federated model was considered but rejected because 30 DAGs in the alliance-wide Gold layer require data from all three countries, and coordinating 30 cross-instance dependencies without a central coordinator would have required a complex message bus that duplicated scheduler functionality.

The per-country executor selection reflects each subsidiary's workload profile. Lithuania inherited SaulėGrid's CeleryExecutor with a Redis broker — it has the highest DAG count (80) and the most consistent workload pattern, where Celery's persistent worker pool provides the best throughput. Estonia's workload is burst-heavy — few DAGs but several involve large Spark job orchestration — making KubernetesExecutor's per-task pod isolation and dynamic scaling the better fit. Latvia's mixed workload led to a hybrid CeleryKubernetesExecutor: lightweight coordination tasks run on Celery workers while heavy processing tasks spin up Kubernetes pods.
When NOT to Use Multi-Instance Topology

Multi-instance topology introduces operational overhead that is only justified when the organization has constraints that a single instance cannot satisfy. If the organization operates in one country with no data residency requirements, a single instance with RBAC provides sufficient isolation at a fraction of the operational cost. If the DAG count is below 150 and the team count is below 5, the scheduling and coordination overhead of multiple instances exceeds the isolation benefit. If the platform team has fewer than 3 engineers, the operational burden of maintaining multiple instances will consume capacity that should be spent on DAG development and pipeline reliability.

blocks.title: Topology is the hardest decision to reverse
blocks.content: Splitting a single instance into multiple instances requires migrating DAGs, splitting the metadata database, reconfiguring connections, and rewriting cross-DAG dependencies. Merging multiple instances into one requires resolving namespace collisions, merging metadata, and reconciling configurations. Both operations take months and carry significant risk. Choose the topology that satisfies your constraints for the next 2–3 years, not the cheapest to deploy this quarter.

A namespace is the naming convention that organizes every DAG on the platform. In Airflow, the namespace is the dag_id — a string that uniquely identifies each DAG across the entire instance. At SaulėGrid's mid-level scale, the convention was simple: dag_{layer}_{domain}, producing names like dag_bronze_meters, dag_silver_transform, and dag_gold_aggregate. With 12 DAGs and one team, this convention was sufficient. At BalticSolar scale, 200+ DAGs across 10 teams and 3 countries require a richer namespace that encodes ownership, country, layer, and domain in the dag_id itself. Without this structure, two teams will inevitably create dag_bronze_meters on the same instance, the platform team will not know which team to contact when a DAG fails, and a new engineer will have no way to discover which DAGs belong to their team.
The Namespace Hierarchy

BalticSolar's namespace follows a four-segment pattern: {country}_{team}_{domain}_{layer}. A Lithuanian data engineering team's Bronze meter ingestion DAG becomes lt_de_meters_bronze. A Latvian analytics team's Gold regulatory reporting DAG becomes lv_analytics_regulatory_gold. The coordinator instance's alliance-wide Gold aggregation DAG becomes eu_platform_alliance_gold. Each segment serves a specific function: country enables per-country filtering in the UI and RBAC, team identifies the owning team for incident routing, domain identifies the data domain for dependency analysis, and layer identifies the medallion layer for pipeline sequencing.
Namespace Validation in CI

The validator is intentionally strict. Every segment must match an allowed value from a controlled vocabulary, not a free-form pattern. This prevents namespace drift — the gradual accumulation of non-standard names that occurs when conventions are documented but not enforced. The controlled vocabulary is maintained in a shared configuration file that the platform team updates through reviewed pull requests. When a new team is created or a new domain is added, the vocabulary is extended through the same review process that governs any platform change.
The Ownership Registry

The namespace encodes ownership in the dag_id, but the platform also needs a machine-readable registry that maps every DAG to its owning team, contact channel, SLA tier, and downstream consumers. BalticSolar's ownership registry is a YAML file in the platform repository that is validated by CI and consumed by the monitoring system, the alerting pipeline, and the governance dashboard. Every DAG that passes namespace validation must also have a corresponding entry in the ownership registry. A DAG without an ownership entry is rejected by CI — unowned DAGs are the organizational equivalent of unowned tables.

# balticsolar-ownership-registry.yaml
# Every production DAG must have an entry. CI rejects DAGs without one.

lt_de_meters_bronze:
  owner_team: lt-data-engineering
  contact_channel: "#lt-de-oncall"
  sla_tier: platinum          # regulatory dependency
  freshness_target: "01:30 EET"
  downstream_consumers:
    - lt_de_meters_silver
    - lt_analytics_regulatory_gold
  runbook_url: "https://wiki.balticsolar.internal/runbooks/lt-meters-bronze"
  created: "2025-09-15"
  last_review: "2026-02-01"   # quarterly review required

lt_de_meters_silver:
  owner_team: lt-data-engineering
  contact_channel: "#lt-de-oncall"
  sla_tier: gold
  freshness_target: "03:00 EET"
  downstream_consumers:
    - lt_analytics_billing_gold
    - lt_analytics_regulatory_gold
    - lt_ml_forecast_gold
    - eu_platform_alliance_gold  # cross-instance consumer
  runbook_url: "https://wiki.balticsolar.internal/runbooks/lt-meters-silver"
  created: "2025-09-15"
  last_review: "2026-02-01"

eu_platform_alliance_gold:
  owner_team: platform-engineering
  contact_channel: "#platform-oncall"
  sla_tier: gold
  freshness_target: "06:00 CET"
  upstream_dependencies:
    - lt_de_meters_silver        # cross-instance
    - lv_de_meters_silver        # cross-instance
    - ee_de_meters_silver        # cross-instance
  downstream_consumers:
    - eu_platform_market_gold
    - eu_analytics_alliance_gold
  runbook_url: "https://wiki.balticsolar.internal/runbooks/alliance-gold"
  created: "2025-11-01"
  last_review: "2026-01-15"

The registry serves three operational functions. First, incident routing: when a DAG fails at 2 AM, the alerting system looks up the owner_team and contact_channel to page the correct on-call engineer without requiring the platform team to maintain a mental mapping of DAGs to teams. Second, impact analysis: when a DAG's SLA is breached, the downstream_consumers field identifies every DAG that will be affected, enabling proactive communication before downstream teams discover the delay themselves. Third, governance: the last_review field enforces the quarterly ownership review — a DAG whose last_review is more than 90 days old generates a warning, and a DAG more than 180 days overdue is flagged for deprecation review.
Ownership Transfer and Team Changes

Teams reorganize. Engineers leave. Companies acquire subsidiaries and merge teams. The ownership model must accommodate these changes without creating orphaned DAGs. BalticSolar's ownership transfer protocol requires the outgoing team to update the registry with the new owner, the incoming team to acknowledge the transfer by updating the contact_channel and completing a runbook review, and the platform team to verify that the transfer is reflected in RBAC permissions. DAGs whose owning team has been dissolved without a transfer are automatically flagged as orphaned and enter a 30-day grace period during which the platform team identifies a new owner. After 30 days, orphaned DAGs are paused with a deprecation notice.
Namespace Design Trade-Offs

The four-segment namespace ({country}_{team}_{domain}_{layer}) optimizes for discoverability and incident routing at the cost of verbosity. DAG IDs like lt_de_meters_bronze are longer than the mid-level dag_bronze_meters, which increases cognitive load when reading code and UI elements. An alternative three-segment design ({country}_{domain}_{layer}) would be shorter but loses the team segment, making incident routing depend on looking up the ownership registry rather than reading the dag_id directly. BalticSolar accepted the verbosity trade-off because the 2 AM incident routing use case is more operationally critical than the code readability concern — an engineer reading code has time to parse a long name, but an on-call engineer receiving an alert needs the routing information immediately.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Namespace conventions are not style preferences — they are operational infrastructure. At BalticSolar, the dag_id determines which team is paged at 2 AM (the country and team segments route the alert), which RBAC role can modify the DAG (country-team combination maps to a role), and which cost center is charged for the DAG's compute (team segment maps to a chargeback account). Enforcing the namespace in CI prevents misrouting, unauthorized access, and misallocated costs at the point of change.

The mid-level track operated three environments: development, staging, and production. This linear dev → staging → prod promotion path works cleanly when one team deploys to one Airflow instance. At BalticSolar scale, the question becomes how many environments, how they map to instances, and how changes flow between them. The naive approach — tripling the mid-level model to give each country its own dev/staging/prod — produces 9 environments plus the coordinator's 3, totaling 12 Airflow deployments. The platform team evaluated this approach and rejected it: 12 environments would consume the entire infrastructure budget and require more operational attention than the 4-person platform team could provide. The environment topology must balance isolation against operational cost.
Two Environment Strategies

left.label: Isolated Environments (9+3)
points: Each country has its own dev, staging, and production. Total: 12 instances. Complete isolation between countries at every stage. Country teams deploy independently. Staging precisely mirrors production per country. But: 12 instances to maintain, high infrastructure cost, configuration drift between environments.

right.label: Shared Development (3+3)
points: All countries share one dev and one staging instance, but each country has its own production. Total: 6 instances. Lower operational cost, centralized development, shared staging validates cross-country interactions. But: shared staging can be disrupted by one country's tests, country-specific config must be parameterized.

blocks.content: BalticSolar chose the shared-development model with a critical modification: the shared staging environment runs all three country configurations simultaneously, using DAG-level environment variables to route each DAG to its country-specific connections and paths. A Lithuanian DAG in staging connects to Lithuania's staging database, not Estonia's, even though both DAGs run on the same Airflow instance. This approach validates cross-country interactions (the coordinator DAG can trigger all three country DAGs in staging) while maintaining country-level configuration isolation.
The Promotion Path

blocks.title: BalticSolar environment promotion flow
blocks.content:
Developer Workstation
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  SHARED DEV INSTANCE            │
│  All countries, all teams       │
│  Relaxed validation             │
└──────────────┬──────────────────┘
               │ PR merge + CI green
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  SHARED STAGING INSTANCE        │
│  All countries, prod config     │
│  Full validation + SLA test     │
└─────┬─────────┬─────────┬───────┘
      │ release │ release │ release
      ▼         ▼         ▼
┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐
│LT PROD│ │LV PROD│ │EE PROD│
└───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘
blocks.caption: Changes flow from developer workstations through shared dev and staging, then fan out to per-country production instances. Lithuania receives every release first as the canary.

visual-recommendation.description: Flow diagram showing the environment promotion path from developer workstation through shared dev and staging to three per-country production instances, with gate descriptions at each transition.

blocks.content: The promotion path has three gates. The first gate is the PR merge with CI validation: namespace validation, ownership registry check, DAG integrity test, and code review approval. This gate runs in minutes and catches structural violations. The second gate is staging validation: the DAG runs in the shared staging environment against staging data sources, the SLA test verifies the DAG completes within its target window, and cross-country dependencies are exercised. This gate runs nightly. The third gate is canary production: Lithuania's production instance receives every release first because it is the largest and most mature deployment. After 48 hours of monitoring with no SLA breaches, the release is promoted to Latvia and Estonia.
Environment Configuration Management

"""BalticSolar environment-aware DAG configuration.

Every DAG reads its configuration from environment-specific
Variables rather than hardcoding paths and connections.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass
from functools import lru_cache

from airflow.models import Variable

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class EnvironmentConfig:
    """Immutable configuration for a specific environment."""
    environment: str              # dev, staging, lt-prod, lv-prod, ee-prod
    country: str                  # lt, lv, ee, eu
    bronze_path: str              # s3://bucket/bronze/{country}/
    silver_path: str              # s3://bucket/silver/{country}/
    gold_path: str                # s3://bucket/gold/{country}/
    warehouse_conn_id: str        # connection ID for the country warehouse
    sftp_conn_id: str             # connection ID for meter data SFTP
    weather_api_conn_id: str      # connection ID for weather forecast API
    regulatory_api_conn_id: str   # connection ID for regulatory submission
    default_pool: str             # execution pool for this environment
    sla_multiplier: float         # 1.0 for prod, 2.0 for staging (relaxed)


@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def get_environment_config() -> EnvironmentConfig:
    """Load environment configuration from Airflow Variables.

    The Variable 'balticsolar_env_config' is set per-instance
    during deployment. Dev and staging instances set it via
    Helm values. Production instances set it via Terraform.
    """
    raw = Variable.get('balticsolar_env_config', deserialize_json=True)
    config = EnvironmentConfig(**raw)
    logger.info(
        "Loaded config: env=%s country=%s", config.environment, config.country,
    )
    return config


def get_connection_id(service: str) -> str:
    """Resolve a connection ID for the current environment.

    Usage: conn_id = get_connection_id('warehouse')
    Returns 'lt_prod_warehouse' in LT production,
    'staging_warehouse_lt' in staging.
    """
    config = get_environment_config()
    conn_map = {
        'warehouse': config.warehouse_conn_id,
        'sftp': config.sftp_conn_id,
        'weather_api': config.weather_api_conn_id,
        'regulatory_api': config.regulatory_api_conn_id,
    }
    if service not in conn_map:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Unknown service '{service}'. Available: {list(conn_map.keys())}"
        )
    return conn_map[service]

The EnvironmentConfig pattern enforces a critical invariant: no DAG file contains any environment-specific value. Paths, connection IDs, pool names, and SLA targets are all resolved at runtime from the instance's environment configuration. This means the same DAG code runs identically in dev, staging, and any country's production instance. The configuration is set once per instance during deployment and remains stable until the next deployment. The lru_cache ensures the configuration is loaded once per interpreter session, avoiding repeated Variable lookups during DAG parsing.
The Staging Isolation Incident

In BalticSolar's second month, the shared staging instance ran a Latvian DAG test that accidentally connected to the Lithuanian production warehouse through a misconfigured connection alias. The DAG wrote 50,000 test rows into a production Silver table. The rows passed quality gates because the test data had valid schemas. The corruption was discovered 6 hours later when the Lithuanian billing team noticed anomalous readings. Recovery required a Delta Lake time-travel restoration and a full re-run of 4 downstream DAGs. After this incident, BalticSolar enforced physical network isolation between staging and production: staging instances cannot reach production databases at the network level, not just at the connection configuration level.

blocks.title: The incident that made isolated production non-negotiable
blocks.content: Network-level isolation between staging and production is not optional at multi-country scale. Connection configuration alone is one misconfigured alias away from cross-environment data corruption. BalticSolar's VPC peering rules now ensure that staging Airflow workers cannot resolve production database hostnames. This is enforced at the infrastructure layer, not the application layer, because infrastructure controls cannot be bypassed by a DAG author's configuration mistake.

When all DAGs run on a single Airflow instance, dependencies are straightforward: Datasets connect producers and consumers within the same scheduler. At BalticSolar, the alliance-wide Gold aggregation DAG runs on the coordinator instance but depends on Silver data produced by DAGs on the Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian instances. Datasets do not work across instances — they are scoped to a single deployment's metadata database. ExternalTaskSensors cannot poll a task on a different instance. The cross-instance dependency problem is one of the most operationally complex aspects of multi-instance topology, and getting it wrong creates cascading failures that are difficult to diagnose because the root cause and the symptom live on different Airflow instances with separate logs.
Three Coordination Patterns

rows: Country instance calls coordinator's DAG trigger endpoint when Silver is ready
Network failure delays trigger. No automatic retry without wrapper.
Simple producer→consumer chains with 1–3 upstream DAGs
Country publishes 'silver_ready' to Redis/RabbitMQ. Coordinator sensor consumes.
Broker unavailability delays all coordination. Ordering issues.
High-volume coordination with many producers and consumers
Coordinator polls each country's data location for freshness
Polling interval creates latency floor. Sensor occupies worker slot.
Fallback when API/broker unavailable or readiness is implicit

blocks.content: BalticSolar uses REST API triggers as the primary coordination mechanism and message broker events as the fallback. When a country instance's Silver DAG completes successfully, its final task calls the coordinator's REST API to trigger the alliance Gold DAG. The trigger includes metadata — the country code, the data interval, and the completion timestamp — that the coordinator uses to track which countries have reported readiness. The coordinator's alliance Gold DAG has a custom sensor that waits for all three country signals before proceeding.
The Cross-Instance Trigger

"""Cross-instance DAG trigger for BalticSolar Alliance.

This task runs as the final step of each country's Silver DAG.
It notifies the coordinator that country Silver data is ready
for alliance-wide Gold aggregation.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from datetime import datetime

import requests
from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.models import Variable

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

COORDINATOR_URL = Variable.get(
    'coordinator_api_url',
    default_var='https://coordinator.balticsolar.internal/api/v1',
)
COORDINATOR_TOKEN = Variable.get('coordinator_api_token', default_var='')


@task(retries=3, retry_delay=30)
def notify_coordinator_silver_ready(
    country: str,
    data_interval_start: str,
    data_interval_end: str,
) -> dict:
    """Notify the coordinator that this country's Silver data is ready.

    Triggers the alliance Gold DAG on the coordinator instance.
    The coordinator waits for all three country notifications
    before starting Gold aggregation.
    """
    payload = {
        'dag_id': 'eu_platform_alliance_gold',
        'conf': {
            'trigger_source': f'{country}_silver_complete',
            'country': country,
            'data_interval_start': data_interval_start,
            'data_interval_end': data_interval_end,
            'notified_at': datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        },
        'note': f'Triggered by {country} Silver completion',
    }

    logger.info(
        "Notifying coordinator: country=%s interval=%s to %s",
        country, data_interval_start, data_interval_end,
    )

    response = requests.post(
        f'{COORDINATOR_URL}/dags/eu_platform_alliance_gold/dagRuns',
        json=payload,
        headers={
            'Authorization': f'Bearer {COORDINATOR_TOKEN}',
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        },
        timeout=30,
    )

    if response.status_code == 409:
        # DagRun already exists — another country triggered first
        logger.info("DagRun already exists for %s. OK.", data_interval_start)
        return {'status': 'already_triggered', 'country': country}

    response.raise_for_status()
    result = response.json()
    logger.info("Coordinator acknowledged: run_id=%s", result.get('dag_run_id'))
    return {'status': 'triggered', 'dag_run_id': result.get('dag_run_id')}

The 409 handling is critical. Because three countries independently notify the coordinator, the first notification creates the DagRun and the subsequent two receive a 409 Conflict because the DagRun already exists for that data interval. The trigger function handles this gracefully — a 409 is a success signal, not an error. The three-retry configuration with 30-second delays ensures that transient network failures do not silently drop a country's notification.
Failure Modes in Cross-Instance Coordination

Cross-instance coordination introduces failure modes that do not exist within a single Airflow deployment. Network partitions between instances can delay or drop trigger notifications. Clock skew between instances can cause the coordinator to misorder country signals. Partial failures — where Lithuania's Silver completes but Latvia's fails — leave the coordinator in a partially-triggered state that requires timeout handling. Coordinator instance failures during the aggregation window mean country signals are accepted but not processed until recovery. Each failure mode requires a specific mitigation: retries with exponential backoff for network transience, UTC timestamps with NTP synchronization for clock skew, configurable timeout with escalation for partial failures, and idempotent triggers for coordinator recovery.
The Coordinator Readiness Sensor

"""Cross-country readiness sensor for the coordinator instance.

Waits for all three country Silver DAGs to report completion
before allowing alliance Gold aggregation to proceed.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from datetime import timedelta

from airflow.sensors.base import BaseSensorOperator
from airflow.models import DagRun
from airflow.utils.session import provide_session

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
REQUIRED_COUNTRIES = {'lt', 'lv', 'ee'}


class AllCountriesReadySensor(BaseSensorOperator):
    """Wait for all required countries to report Silver readiness."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        required_countries: set[str] = REQUIRED_COUNTRIES,
        sla_timeout: timedelta = timedelta(hours=4),
        **kwargs,
    ):
        super().__init__(mode='reschedule', **kwargs)
        self.required_countries = required_countries
        self.sla_timeout = sla_timeout

    @provide_session
    def poke(self, context, session=None) -> bool:
        dag_run: DagRun = context['dag_run']
        conf = dag_run.conf or {}

        reported = set()
        trigger_source = conf.get('trigger_source', '')
        if '_silver_complete' in trigger_source:
            reported.add(trigger_source.replace('_silver_complete', ''))

        for country in self.required_countries:
            if conf.get(f'{country}_silver_ready'):
                reported.add(country)

        missing = self.required_countries - reported

        if not missing:
            logger.info("All countries ready: %s", self.required_countries)
            return True

        logger.info("Waiting: %s (reported: %s)", missing, reported)
        return False

The sensor uses reschedule mode rather than poke mode to avoid occupying a worker slot during the entire wait period. In poke mode, the sensor holds a worker slot for potentially hours while waiting for the slowest country. In reschedule mode, the sensor checks, releases the slot, and the scheduler re-queues it after the poke_interval. This distinction matters because the coordinator instance also runs 30 other alliance-wide DAGs — holding worker slots for long-running sensors would reduce slots available for actual work. The sla_timeout parameter defines the maximum wait: if any country has not reported within 4 hours, the platform team is paged.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Cross-instance coordination should be explicit, auditable, and timeout-bounded. Explicit: every trigger is a deliberate API call with logged metadata, not an implicit side effect. Auditable: the coordinator's conf contains a complete record of which countries reported, when, and for what data interval. Timeout-bounded: no dependency waits indefinitely — a 4-hour timeout with escalation is better than a silent 12-hour wait that misses both the SLA and the escalation window.

The mid-level track managed Airflow configuration through a single airflow.cfg file with environment-specific overrides via environment variables. This approach works when one team manages one instance. At BalticSolar scale, configuration becomes an architectural concern. Three country instances share some configuration (the alliance-wide DAG parsing timeout, the default retry policy, the logging format) but diverge on others (executor type, worker count, connection pool size, timezone). The coordinator has its own profile. Ten teams have conflicting preferences: the ML team wants larger XCom payloads, the regulatory team wants stricter SLA checking, and the analytics team wants higher concurrency. Without a configuration architecture, each instance drifts independently, team preferences override platform standards, and debugging becomes impossible because no one can state with certainty what the current configuration is.
The Configuration Hierarchy

blocks.title: BalticSolar configuration hierarchy
blocks.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ALLIANCE DEFAULTS  (lowest precedence)          │
│  DAG parsing timeout, retry policy, log format    │
└───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
                        │ overridden by
┌───────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
│  COUNTRY OVERRIDES                               │
│  Executor type, timezone, worker count,           │
│  country-specific connection defaults              │
└───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
                        │ overridden by
┌───────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
│  INSTANCE OVERRIDES  (highest precedence)        │
│  Environment-specific secrets, pool sizes,         │
│  infrastructure endpoints                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: Configuration resolves from three layers: alliance defaults, country overrides, and instance-specific overrides. Higher layers take precedence. All layers are version-controlled in Git.

visual-recommendation.description: Layered configuration hierarchy showing three tiers with example parameters at each level and override arrows between them.

blocks.content: The hierarchy follows the same override pattern as Helm values: alliance defaults provide the baseline, country overrides replace specific values for each country, and instance overrides handle environment-specific infrastructure details. The key constraint is that every layer is version-controlled in Git. Alliance defaults live in config/alliance/defaults.yaml. Country overrides live in config/countries/{lt,lv,ee}/overrides.yaml. Instance overrides live in config/instances/{env}/overrides.yaml. A deployment pipeline merges the three layers, validates the result against a configuration schema, and deploys the merged configuration. No value is set manually on any instance — Git is the single source of truth.
Configuration Drift Detection

"""Configuration drift detection for BalticSolar Airflow instances.

Runs daily on the coordinator. Compares expected config (from Git)
against actual config reported by each country instance's API.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass

import requests
import yaml
from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.models import Variable

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

MONITORED_KEYS = [
    'core.parallelism',
    'core.max_active_tasks_per_dag',
    'core.max_active_runs_per_dag',
    'scheduler.parsing_processes',
    'scheduler.min_file_process_interval',
    'celery.worker_concurrency',
    'core.dag_file_processor_timeout',
]


@dataclass
class DriftResult:
    instance: str
    key: str
    expected: str
    actual: str


@task
def load_expected_config(instance_name: str) -> dict:
    """Load expected configuration for an instance from Git."""
    config_path = Variable.get('config_repo_path')
    with open(f'{config_path}/rendered/{instance_name}.yaml') as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)


@task
def fetch_actual_config(instance_url: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch running configuration from the instance API."""
    token = Variable.get(f'api_token_{instance_url}')
    response = requests.get(
        f'{instance_url}/api/v1/config',
        headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'},
        timeout=30,
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    config_map = {}
    for section in response.json().get('sections', []):
        for option in section.get('options', []):
            config_map[f"{section['name']}.{option['key']}"] = option['value']
    return config_map


@task
def detect_drift(
    instance_name: str, expected: dict, actual: dict,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Compare expected vs actual config and report drift."""
    drifts = []
    for key in MONITORED_KEYS:
        exp = str(expected.get(key, 'NOT_SET'))
        act = str(actual.get(key, 'NOT_SET'))
        if exp != act:
            drifts.append({
                'instance': instance_name, 'key': key,
                'expected': exp, 'actual': act,
            })
            logger.warning("Drift: %s.%s exp=%s act=%s", instance_name, key, exp, act)
    return drifts

The drift detection DAG checks each country instance's running configuration against the expected configuration from Git. Any divergence indicates that someone modified configuration directly on the instance — bypassing GitOps, peer review, and schema validation. Drift is not necessarily a crisis, but untracked drift erodes configuration-as-code reliability. The alert gives the platform team 24 hours to revert or formalize the change. After 48 hours of unresolved drift, the alert escalates to the alliance platform lead.
Conflict Resolution for Team Preferences

When 10 teams share a platform, configuration conflicts are inevitable. The ML team wants max_active_tasks_per_dag set to 32 for parallel model training DAGs, but the regulatory team wants it capped at 8 to prevent their single-threaded submission DAGs from consuming all worker slots. BalticSolar resolves this through DAG-level overrides: the platform's alliance default is 16, but individual DAGs can override it via the max_active_tasks parameter on the DAG object. Team-level preferences that affect only their own DAGs are resolved at the DAG level. Platform-level parameters that affect all DAGs (like scheduler.parsing_processes) are decided by the platform team after consulting affected teams, and the decision is documented in an Architecture Decision Record.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Configuration-as-code is only as reliable as its enforcement. If engineers can bypass the Git pipeline by editing airflow.cfg directly, the repository degrades from a source of truth to a suggestion. The drift detection system converts 'we should use Git for configuration' from a team agreement into a monitored invariant with automated alerts and escalation. The goal is not to prevent emergency changes — the goal is to ensure they are tracked and reconciled within 48 hours.

At mid-level scale, SaulėGrid's DAG repository was a single directory with 12 Python files and a shared utilities module. Any engineer could find any DAG by scanning the directory listing. At 200+ DAGs across 10 teams, the repository structure becomes an architectural decision that affects code discoverability, CI/CD pipeline design, team autonomy, and deployment granularity. The wrong structure creates merge conflicts between unrelated teams, CI pipelines that test every DAG on every commit, and deployment processes where a Lithuanian DAG change triggers an Estonian redeployment. The right structure gives each team a clear workspace while maintaining shared standards that the platform team enforces.
Monorepo vs Multi-Repo

left.label: Monorepo (All DAGs in One Repository)
points: Single source of truth for all DAG code. Shared libraries available to all teams without versioning overhead. Platform-wide CI enforces standards across every DAG. Atomic commits across cross-team dependencies. But: merge conflicts between unrelated teams, CI that runs all tests on every commit, single CODEOWNERS file that becomes complex at 10 teams.

right.label: Multi-Repo (One Repository Per Team)
points: Complete team autonomy over their repository. Independent CI pipelines. No merge conflicts between teams. Each team controls their release cadence. But: shared library distribution requires packaging and versioning, cross-team DAG references require coordination, platform-wide standards enforcement requires per-repo CI configuration that can drift.

blocks.content: BalticSolar chose the monorepo with team-owned directories. The decision was driven by the cross-country DAG dependency pattern: the 30 coordinator DAGs reference utilities and schemas from country-specific DAGs, and maintaining those references across separate repositories would require a package management layer that adds complexity without adding value. The monorepo's merge conflict risk is mitigated by team directory ownership — CODEOWNERS ensures that a Lithuanian team's PR requires only Lithuanian reviewers, not the entire alliance.
Directory Structure

balticsolar-airflow/
├── dags/
│   ├── lt/                          # Lithuanian country instance DAGs
│   │   ├── de/                      # LT data engineering team
│   │   │   ├── meters/              # meters domain
│   │   │   │   ├── lt_de_meters_bronze.py
│   │   │   │   ├── lt_de_meters_silver.py
│   │   │   │   └── lt_de_meters_gold.py
│   │   │   ├── scada/
│   │   │   └── weather/
│   │   ├── analytics/               # LT analytics team
│   │   └── ml/                      # LT ML team
│   ├── lv/                          # Latvian country instance DAGs
│   │   ├── de/
│   │   ├── analytics/
│   │   └── regulatory/
│   ├── ee/                          # Estonian country instance DAGs
│   │   └── ...
│   └── eu/                          # Coordinator instance DAGs
│       └── platform/
│           ├── alliance/
│           └── market/
├── libs/
│   ├── balticsolar_common/          # Shared utilities (all teams)
│   │   ├── config.py                # EnvironmentConfig (lesson 4)
│   │   ├── namespace.py             # DAG ID validation (lesson 3)
│   │   └── notifications.py         # Cross-instance triggers (lesson 5)
│   └── country_specific/
│       ├── lt_regulatory/           # Lithuanian VERT integration
│       ├── lv_regulatory/           # Latvian SPRK integration
│       └── ee_regulatory/           # Estonian Konkurentsi integration
├── config/
│   ├── alliance/defaults.yaml
│   ├── countries/{lt,lv,ee}/overrides.yaml
│   └── instances/{env}/overrides.yaml
├── tests/
│   ├── unit/
│   ├── integration/
│   └── dag_integrity/
├── .github/
│   ├── CODEOWNERS
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── ci.yaml
│       └── deploy.yaml
└── ownership-registry.yaml

The directory structure mirrors the namespace hierarchy: dags/{country}/{team}/{domain}/{dag_file}.py. This makes discoverability trivial — an engineer looking for the Latvian analytics team's regulatory Gold DAG navigates to dags/lv/analytics/regulatory/. The libs/ directory contains shared utilities that any team can import, while country_specific/ libraries contain integrations that are only relevant to one country's regulatory system. The CODEOWNERS file maps each directory to the responsible team, ensuring that PRs touching dags/lt/ require approval from the Lithuanian team, not the Estonian team.
CI/CD With Selective Testing

A monorepo with 200+ DAGs cannot run every test on every commit — the CI would take hours. BalticSolar's CI pipeline uses path-based filtering to run only the tests affected by each PR. A change to dags/lt/de/meters/ triggers Lithuanian meter DAG tests and the dag_integrity tests for the LT instance, but not Estonian or Latvian tests. A change to libs/balticsolar_common/ triggers the full test suite because shared utilities affect all teams. A change to config/ triggers configuration schema validation and drift detection but not DAG tests. This selective approach keeps CI under 10 minutes for team-specific changes while maintaining full coverage for platform-wide changes.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Repository structure is deployment architecture. At BalticSolar, the directory tree determines which tests run (path-based CI filtering), who reviews (CODEOWNERS), which instance receives the deployment (country directory maps to country instance), and how fast CI completes (selective testing). A flat repository where all DAGs share one directory would make all of these operations slower and more error-prone. Structure the repository to match your organizational and deployment boundaries.

When SaulėGrid operated a single Airflow instance for one team, infrastructure cost was a single line item in the team's cloud budget. At BalticSolar scale, the orchestration platform costs are shared across 10 teams in 3 countries, and the question of who pays for what becomes an organizational problem. Without cost allocation, the platform team absorbs all infrastructure costs and cannot demonstrate the ROI of platform improvements. Teams have no incentive to optimize their DAGs because the cost is invisible to them — a DAG that runs for 4 hours consuming 8 workers costs the same to the team as a DAG that runs for 10 minutes consuming 1 worker, because neither team sees the bill.
The Chargeback Model

BalticSolar allocates orchestration costs at the team level using a three-component model: compute cost (worker CPU-hours consumed by each team's tasks), storage cost (metadata database and log storage attributable to each team's DAGs), and coordination cost (the platform team's operational overhead, distributed equally across the 10 teams as a fixed platform fee). Compute cost is the largest component and the most directly attributable — each task runs in a worker process or pod that can be tagged with the owning team's identifier using the task's pool or queue assignment.
Task-Level Cost Tagging

"""BalticSolar cost tagging for DAG tasks.

Each task is tagged with the owning team's identifier through
Airflow's pool and label system. The cost allocation pipeline
reads these tags from the metadata database and executor logs
to attribute compute hours to the correct team.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.utils.dates import days_ago

# Team pool mapping — each team has a dedicated pool
# that both limits concurrency and enables cost tracking
TEAM_POOLS = {
    'lt-de': 'pool_lt_de',          # 20 slots
    'lt-analytics': 'pool_lt_analytics',  # 12 slots
    'lt-ml': 'pool_lt_ml',          # 8 slots
    'lv-de': 'pool_lv_de',          # 15 slots
    'platform': 'pool_platform',     # 10 slots
    # ... remaining teams
}


@dag(
    dag_id='lt_de_meters_bronze',
    schedule='@daily',
    start_date=days_ago(1),
    tags=['lt', 'de', 'meters', 'bronze', 'cost:lt-de'],
    default_args={
        'pool': TEAM_POOLS['lt-de'],
        'owner': 'lt-data-engineering',
        'retries': 3,
        'retry_delay': 60,
    },
)
def lt_de_meters_bronze():
    """Bronze meter ingestion for Lithuanian meters."""

    @task
    def ingest_meters():
        # Task inherits pool='pool_lt_de' from default_args
        # Cost allocation reads pool assignment from task_instance table
        pass

    @task
    def validate_schema():
        pass

    @task
    def update_registry():
        pass

    ingest_meters() >> validate_schema() >> update_registry()


lt_de_meters_bronze()

The pool assignment serves dual purpose: it limits each team's concurrent task count (preventing any single team from monopolizing the worker fleet) and it provides the cost attribution tag. The cost allocation pipeline runs weekly on the coordinator instance, querying each country instance's metadata database for task_instance records, aggregating CPU-hours by pool, and mapping pools to teams via the TEAM_POOLS configuration. The cost report is published to the alliance finance dashboard and reviewed in the monthly infrastructure cost meeting.
The Cost Anomaly Detection System

Cost allocation without anomaly detection is a retrospective exercise — the team discovers their DAGs cost twice as much as last month only when the monthly report arrives. BalticSolar's cost anomaly detection runs daily, comparing each team's rolling 7-day compute cost against their 30-day baseline. A team whose daily compute cost exceeds 150% of their baseline triggers a notification to the team lead and the platform team. This early warning catches runaway DAGs (a DAG stuck in a retry loop consuming workers), misconfigured schedules (a DAG accidentally set to run every minute instead of every hour), and legitimate workload changes that need budget approval before the monthly report.

blocks.title: Cost allocation is organizational, not technical
blocks.content: The technical implementation of cost tagging is straightforward — pools, tags, and metadata queries. The organizational challenge is getting 10 teams to accept the chargeback model, agree on the cost allocation methodology, and act on the monthly report. BalticSolar's model was negotiated over 3 months and revised twice before all teams accepted it. The key compromise: the platform team absorbs the first 20% of any team's cost overrun as an 'innovation buffer' — this gives teams room to experiment without immediate budget pressure while still making costs visible.

The mid-level track built observability around a single Airflow instance: Grafana dashboards for DAG success rates, task duration trends, and scheduler metrics pulled from Airflow's built-in StatsD emitter. At BalticSolar scale, the observability challenge multiplies: four Airflow instances emit metrics independently, each country team monitors their own DAGs, the platform team needs alliance-wide visibility, and the coordinator's cross-instance metrics require aggregation from multiple sources. Without a unified observability architecture, the platform team discovers cross-country incidents by receiving separate alerts from three country teams rather than seeing the correlated failure pattern on a single dashboard.
The Observability Stack

blocks.title: BalticSolar observability architecture
blocks.caption: All four Airflow instances push StatsD metrics to a central Prometheus instance. Grafana provides dashboards, PagerDuty handles critical alerts, and Slack delivers informational notifications.

visual-recommendation.description: Architecture diagram showing four Airflow instances pushing metrics to central Prometheus, with Grafana dashboards, PagerDuty alerts, and Slack notifications as consumers.

blocks.content: The observability architecture centers on metric aggregation: each Airflow instance emits StatsD metrics tagged with the instance name (lt, lv, ee, coordinator). A central Prometheus instance scrapes all four StatsD exporters and stores the time-series data with instance labels. Grafana dashboards query Prometheus with label filters to show per-instance, per-country, and alliance-wide views. PagerDuty receives critical alerts (SLA breaches, scheduler failures, worker fleet health) through Prometheus Alertmanager rules. Slack receives informational notifications (DAG completions, cost anomalies, ownership review reminders) through a webhook integration.
The Three Dashboard Layers

rows: Total DAG success rate, cross-country SLA achievement, alliance cost trend, instance health status
Per-DAG success rate, task duration P95, scheduling delay, worker utilization, metadata DB latency
Team's DAGs only: success/failure history, SLA achievement, cost attribution, pending backfills

blocks.content: The three layers correspond to three operational questions. The alliance overview answers 'is the platform healthy?' — a single screen that the platform lead checks every morning. The country instance dashboard answers 'what is happening in my country?' — the dashboard that each country's on-call engineer monitors during their shift. The team view answers 'how are my DAGs performing?' — the dashboard that each team lead reviews in their weekly standup. Each layer filters the same underlying Prometheus metrics using different label combinations, so the data is consistent across all views.
Cross-Instance Correlation

The most valuable observability feature at BalticSolar scale is cross-instance correlation: when the alliance Gold DAG fails, the platform team needs to immediately determine whether the failure originated in Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, or the coordinator itself. The correlation dashboard shows the timeline of events across all four instances for a given data interval — when each country's Bronze started, when Silver completed, when the coordinator trigger was received, and where the chain broke. This correlation view reduced the mean time to identify the failing instance from 25 minutes (checking each instance's UI manually) to under 2 minutes (reading the correlation timeline).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Observability at multi-instance scale is not about having more dashboards — it is about having dashboards that correlate across instances. A per-instance dashboard that shows 'all green' on the Lithuanian instance while the coordinator is failing due to a missing Lithuanian signal is worse than no dashboard, because it creates false confidence. The platform dashboard must show the causal chain across instances, not just the health of each instance in isolation.

This lesson synthesizes the entire module into BalticSolar Alliance's complete platform architecture. Each preceding lesson addressed one architectural dimension — topology, namespace, environments, cross-country coordination, configuration, repository structure, cost allocation, and observability. This lesson traces how those dimensions interact, where the trade-offs between them create tension, and how the governance processes hold the architecture together. The synthesis is structured as a design document that a new platform engineer could read on their first day to understand the platform's architecture, the decisions behind it, and the constraints that govern changes.
The Architecture Summary

rows: Verbose dag_ids vs immediate ownership visibility
Team autonomy vs shared code access
Central infrastructure cost vs distributed monitoring

blocks.content: How the Dimensions Interact

The architecture's dimensions are not independent — a decision in one dimension constrains choices in others. The hybrid topology (lesson 2) requires cross-instance coordination (lesson 5), which requires the coordinator's REST API triggers, which require the environment configuration system (lesson 4) to resolve the correct coordinator URL per environment. The namespace convention (lesson 3) encodes the team segment that the cost allocation system (lesson 8) uses for chargeback, which means changing the namespace convention requires updating the cost pipeline. The repository structure (lesson 7) mirrors the namespace hierarchy, which means adding a new team to the namespace vocabulary requires creating the corresponding directory, updating CODEOWNERS, and provisioning the team's pool for cost tagging. These interdependencies are not accidental — they are the mechanism by which the architecture maintains consistency. A change that touches multiple dimensions triggers the governance review process.
The Governance Process

Architectural decisions are not made by the platform team alone. BalticSolar's governance process uses Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for any change that affects more than one dimension of the platform architecture. An ADR documents the decision context (what problem are we solving?), the options considered (what alternatives were evaluated?), the decision (what did we choose?), and the consequences (what trade-offs did we accept?). ADRs are reviewed by the platform team and at least one representative from each affected country team. The ADR repository is the authoritative record of how the architecture evolved and why each decision was made — a new engineer reads the ADR history to understand not just what the architecture is but why it is not the obvious alternative.
The Quarterly Architecture Review

Every quarter, the platform team convenes the architecture review with representatives from all 10 teams. The review evaluates the architecture against the current operational reality: are the DAG count and team count still within the topology's design capacity? Has any country's workload pattern shifted enough to reconsider the executor choice? Are the SLA targets still achievable with the current scheduling governance? Has the cost allocation model created perverse incentives (teams splitting DAGs to reduce per-DAG cost visibility)? The review produces a prioritized list of architectural adjustments, each tracked as an ADR. The review is not a ceremony — it is the mechanism that prevents the architecture from ossifying. A platform that does not evolve with its workload will eventually be replaced by one that does.
What This Module Did NOT Cover

This module focused on the organizational architecture — the structures and processes that govern the platform. The remaining modules address the technical architecture within this organizational framework. Module 2 covers DAG factories that generate the 200+ DAGs this module governs. Module 3 covers the scheduling architecture that allocates the resources this module budgets. Module 4 covers the executors that run on the instances this module deployed. Module 5 covers the integration layer that connects to the external systems this module coordinates across. Modules 6 and 7 go inside the scheduler and metadata database that this module configured. Module 8 covers the multi-tenant isolation that this module's RBAC and namespace conventions enable. Module 9 extends this module's observability design into full incident architecture. And Module 10 addresses the governance framework that sustains this module's architecture over years.

blocks.title: The senior-level promise
blocks.content: Platform architecture is not a one-time design exercise — it is an ongoing practice of evaluating trade-offs, monitoring the architecture's fitness for the current workload, and evolving the architecture when the workload outgrows the design. The decisions in this module will need revision as BalticSolar adds countries, teams, and DAGs. The architecture's value is not that it is optimal today — it is that it is documented, governed, and structured for deliberate evolution rather than unplanned drift.

chapters.title: DAG Factories and Config-Driven Orchestration
chapters.description: Design and implement DAG factories that generate production DAGs from configuration — replacing hand-crafted DAG files with config-driven orchestration that scales to 200+ pipelines with consistent structure, testing, and governance.

blocks.content: At SaulėGrid's mid-level scale, every DAG was a hand-crafted Python file. An engineer opened their editor, wrote a DAG definition, added tasks, configured schedules and dependencies, and deployed the file to the DAGs directory. With 12 DAGs owned by 3 teams, this approach was manageable — each engineer knew every DAG, each DAG followed the team's informal conventions, and a cross-cutting change (adding a new alerting callback, for example) required editing 12 files. At BalticSolar Alliance's scale of 200+ DAGs across 10 teams, hand-crafted DAGs create four compounding problems that the mid-level approach cannot solve: inconsistency, maintenance burden, onboarding friction, and audit difficulty. Each problem alone is manageable. Together, they create an operational crisis that consumes more engineering time than the pipelines themselves produce value.
The Four Problems
Inconsistency

Each of BalticSolar's 10 teams developed their own DAG conventions during the first months after the alliance merger. The Lithuanian data engineering team used the @dag decorator with default_args dictionaries. The Latvian team used the classic DAG() context manager with inline arguments. The Estonian team used a hybrid approach with factory functions that were not standardized. Retry policies varied from 1 to 5 retries with delays ranging from 30 seconds to 10 minutes. Alerting callbacks existed on some DAGs but not others. SLA definitions used different timeout calculations. The inconsistency was not a matter of style — it affected operational reliability. When the platform team investigated a cross-country SLA breach, they discovered that the Lithuanian DAG had SLA monitoring enabled but the Estonian DAG feeding it did not, so the upstream failure was invisible until it cascaded downstream.
Maintenance Burden

In the alliance's sixth month, a security audit required adding audit logging to every task callback. With 200+ hand-crafted DAGs, this meant editing 200+ files, each with slightly different callback patterns. The platform team estimated 3 weeks of engineering time for the change. Two months later, a new monitoring requirement added another cross-cutting change. Then a connection naming convention update. Each change required touching every DAG file, testing each modification, and deploying through the CI/CD pipeline. The cumulative maintenance burden exceeded the time spent building new pipelines — the platform was consuming itself.
Onboarding Friction

A new engineer joining the Latvian analytics team needed 2–3 weeks to learn the team's specific DAG patterns before they could write their first production DAG. Each team's conventions were documented in team wikis that were often outdated and always incomplete. The mental overhead of learning 'how our team writes DAGs' before learning 'how to solve the data problem' delayed productivity and created a barrier to cross-team collaboration. An engineer who understood Lithuanian DAG patterns could not easily contribute to Estonian DAGs because the patterns were different in ways that were not visible until the code was read line by line.
Audit Difficulty

BalticSolar's regulatory compliance requires that all production DAGs follow specific patterns: audit logging on every task, SLA monitoring with country-specific thresholds, connection usage through the approved Secrets Backend, and data residency compliance for cross-country data movement. Verifying these requirements across 200+ hand-crafted DAGs required manual code review — a reviewer opening each DAG file and checking for the presence of each required pattern. The quarterly audit consumed 2 weeks of a senior engineer's time and still missed violations because the patterns were encoded in code, not in a verifiable schema.
The Tipping Point

rows: Every change takes longer than the next change arrives
New engineer time to first DAG

blocks.content: The tipping point arrived when the platform team calculated that 60% of their engineering time was spent on cross-cutting DAG maintenance rather than platform improvement. The maintenance burden was not caused by bad engineering — it was caused by a pattern that does not scale. Hand-crafted DAGs are the artisanal approach to orchestration: each DAG is individually shaped, which produces quality at small scale but creates unsustainable variety at large scale. The solution is industrialization: a factory that produces DAGs from standardized specifications, guaranteeing consistency, enabling cross-cutting changes through template updates, accelerating onboarding through configuration rather than code, and making audits mechanical rather than manual.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The DAG factory is not a convenience tool — it is an architectural response to the maintenance scaling problem. The factory converts cross-cutting changes from O(n) operations (edit every DAG file) to O(1) operations (edit the factory template). It converts audit compliance from manual review to schema validation. It converts onboarding from 'learn our code patterns' to 'learn our config schema.' The factory's value is proportional to the DAG count: below 30 DAGs, the factory's overhead exceeds its savings. Above 100, the factory is the only sustainable approach.

A DAG factory is a Python function that accepts a configuration object — typically loaded from a YAML file — and returns a fully constructed Airflow DAG with tasks, dependencies, schedules, callbacks, and metadata. The factory is the bridge between declarative configuration (what the pipeline should do) and imperative Airflow code (how the pipeline is constructed). The fundamental principle is that the factory is a compiler: it translates a pipeline specification into Airflow objects, just as a programming language compiler translates source code into machine instructions. The pipeline team writes the specification; the platform team writes the compiler. Neither needs to understand the other's domain in detail.
The Factory Function Signature

"""BalticSolar DAG factory — the core pattern.

The factory accepts a PipelineConfig dataclass and returns
a fully constructed DAG. Pipeline teams never write DAG code
directly — they write YAML configs that the factory compiles.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import timedelta
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

import yaml
from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.models import Variable

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class SourceConfig:
    """Configuration for a single data source."""
    name: str
    connection_id: str
    source_type: str                  # sftp, api, database, s3
    source_path: str                  # path, URL, or query
    file_format: str = 'csv'          # csv, parquet, json
    schema_version: int = 1


@dataclass
class TargetConfig:
    """Configuration for the pipeline's output target."""
    path: str                         # s3://bucket/layer/domain/
    format: str = 'delta'             # delta, parquet
    partition_columns: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    mode: str = 'append'              # append, overwrite, merge


@dataclass
class PipelineConfig:
    """Complete pipeline specification loaded from YAML."""
    dag_id: str
    owner_team: str
    country: str
    domain: str
    layer: str
    schedule: str                     # cron, Dataset URI, or timetable ref
    sources: list[SourceConfig]
    target: TargetConfig
    sla_minutes: int = 60
    retries: int = 3
    retry_delay_seconds: int = 300
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    alerts: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    quality_checks: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    @classmethod
    def from_yaml(cls, path: Path) -> 'PipelineConfig':
        """Load a pipeline config from a YAML file."""
        with open(path) as f:
            raw = yaml.safe_load(f)
        raw['sources'] = [SourceConfig(**s) for s in raw.get('sources', [])]
        raw['target'] = TargetConfig(**raw.get('target', {}))
        return cls(**raw)

The PipelineConfig dataclass is the contract between pipeline teams and the platform team. Pipeline teams fill in the fields — sources, target, schedule, SLA — without needing to know how Airflow constructs DAGs internally. The platform team owns the factory function that converts PipelineConfig into a DAG. The dataclass provides type checking, default values, and clear documentation of every configurable parameter. The from_yaml class method is the entry point: the factory scans a configuration directory, loads each YAML file into a PipelineConfig, and calls the factory function for each one.
The Factory Function

The factory function creates a standard five-task chain: ingest, validate, transform, quality check, notify. Every generated DAG follows this structure regardless of the data domain or the owning team. The structure is not arbitrary — it encodes BalticSolar's operational requirements: ingestion must be separated from transformation so that ingestion failures can be retried without re-running transformations. Schema validation must happen before transformation so that schema-breaking source changes are caught early. Quality checks must run on the output before notifications are sent. This standardization is the factory's primary value — it guarantees that every pipeline follows the same operational pattern.
The Registration Loop

"""DAG factory registration — scans config directory and generates DAGs.

This file is placed in the Airflow DAGs directory. The scheduler
discovers it during parsing and executes it, which causes the
factory to generate one DAG per YAML config file.
"""
from pathlib import Path

# Config directory is set per-instance via environment variable
CONFIG_DIR = Path(Variable.get(
    'factory_config_dir',
    default_var='/opt/airflow/configs/pipelines',
))

def register_all_dags():
    """Scan the config directory and create DAGs for each YAML file."""
    if not CONFIG_DIR.exists():
        logger.warning("Config directory %s does not exist", CONFIG_DIR)
        return

    config_files = sorted(CONFIG_DIR.glob('**/*.yaml'))
    logger.info("Found %d pipeline configs in %s", len(config_files), CONFIG_DIR)

    for config_path in config_files:
        try:
            config = PipelineConfig.from_yaml(config_path)
            create_dag(config)
            logger.debug("Registered DAG: %s", config.dag_id)
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error(
                "Failed to create DAG from %s: %s",
                config_path, e,
                exc_info=True,
            )
            # Do not raise — one bad config should not prevent
            # other DAGs from being registered

register_all_dags()

The registration loop is the mechanism that makes factory-generated DAGs visible to the scheduler. The scheduler discovers the factory file during parsing, executes it, and the register_all_dags function scans the configuration directory for YAML files. Each YAML file becomes one DAG. The error handling is deliberate: if one config file is malformed, the factory logs the error and continues to the next file. A single broken config must not prevent 199 other DAGs from being registered — this is the production resilience principle that separates a factory from a script. The factory tags every generated DAG with factory:v{VERSION}, enabling the platform team to track which factory version produced each running DAG and to identify DAGs that need re-generation after a factory update.

blocks.title: The factory is a compiler
blocks.content: The mental model for a DAG factory is a compiler, not a template engine. A template engine fills in blanks in a pre-existing structure. A compiler transforms a high-level specification into a lower-level representation with optimization, validation, and error reporting. The factory validates the configuration against the schema (type checking), generates the optimal task graph for the specified sources (optimization), and reports configuration errors with actionable messages (error reporting). Treating the factory as a compiler rather than a template shapes every design decision in this module.

The configuration schema is the factory's public API. It defines what pipeline teams can express, what the factory guarantees, and what the validation system enforces. A well-designed schema makes common pipelines trivial to configure (a Bronze ingestion DAG requires 10–15 lines of YAML), uncommon pipelines possible (a multi-source Silver transformation with conditional branches requires 40–60 lines), and invalid pipelines impossible (a config that references a non-existent connection is rejected before it reaches the scheduler). The schema design tension is between expressiveness and simplicity: a schema that can express every possible pipeline is as complex as writing Python directly, while a schema that only supports simple pipelines forces teams to bypass the factory for anything non-trivial.
BalticSolar's Config Schema

# Pipeline config schema — version 3
# This schema defines every field a pipeline team can configure.
# Fields marked 'required' must be present. Fields with defaults
# can be omitted. The factory validates every config against
# this schema before generating the DAG.

# === Identity (required) ===
dag_id: lt_de_meters_bronze         # Must match namespace convention
owner_team: lt-data-engineering      # Must exist in ownership registry
country: lt                          # lt, lv, ee, or eu
domain: meters                       # Must be in allowed domains
layer: bronze                        # bronze, silver, gold

# === Schedule (required) ===
schedule: "@daily"                   # Cron, Dataset URI, or timetable ref
# Alternative schedule formats:
