The compliance report generator runs on the coordinator instance as a quarterly DAG triggered by the compliance team. The report queries the centralized compliance warehouse (not the individual country metadata databases) to produce country-specific metrics that feed into the regulatory filings. The metric definitions are standardized across all three regulators: total_dag_runs and total_task_executions demonstrate operational volume, failed_task_count demonstrates incident rate, manual_interventions demonstrates human oversight, and rbac_violations and residency_violations demonstrate that isolation controls are effective (the target is zero for both). The audit_completeness_pct metric verifies that the export pipeline captured all expected audit events — a value below 99% triggers an investigation into audit data loss.

blocks.title: Compliance is not a quarterly exercise
blocks.content: The quarterly compliance review produces the regulatory report, but compliance itself is continuous. Every DAG deployment that includes access_control is a compliance action. Every credential rotation verified by a test run is a compliance action. Every CI pipeline that validates namespace conventions is a compliance action. The quarterly review aggregates these continuous actions into a reportable summary. If compliance work only happens during the quarterly review, the platform has already accumulated 3 months of potential violations that the review discovers rather than prevents.

Every team on the BalticSolar platform has a lifecycle: onboarding when the team joins the platform, steady-state governance during active operation, and offboarding when the team is dissolved or migrated to a different system. Without structured lifecycle management, onboarding is ad-hoc (the new team waits weeks for access while the platform team manually creates resources), steady-state is ungoverned (permissions drift, unused connections accumulate, DAG counts grow without review), and offboarding is incomplete (orphaned DAGs, zombie connections, and stale RBAC entries persist indefinitely). BalticSolar's tenant lifecycle process standardizes each phase into repeatable, auditable procedures.
The Onboarding Checklist

items: Namespace registration: add the team's namespace segment to the DAG factory's allowed vocabulary (Module 1, lesson 3). The team's namespace must be unique, approved by the platform team, and documented in the vocabulary registry.

RBAC role creation: create the team's custom role based on the appropriate base role template (lesson 2). Apply DAG-level access control patterns for the team's namespace. Assign team members to the role.

Vault secrets provisioning: create the team's secrets path in the appropriate country secret engine. Provision initial connection credentials. Verify that the team's Airflow instance token can read the new path.
DAG directory creation: create the team's directory in the monorepo following the convention dags/{country}/{team}/ (Module 1, lesson 7). Update CODEOWNERS to assign the team as the directory owner.
Pool allocation: create the team's entry in the cost allocation model (Module 1, lesson 8). Assign the team to the appropriate pool tier based on their SLA requirements (Module 3, lesson 3).
Monitoring setup: add the team's DAGs to the observability dashboard (Module 1, lesson 9). Configure alerting routes for the team's on-call channel. Verify that the team receives a test alert.

Onboarding documentation: provide the team with the platform onboarding guide covering namespace conventions, factory config schema, CI/CD pipeline usage, on-call expectations, and escalation procedures.
Verification run: the team deploys their first DAG (factory-generated or hand-crafted) through the full CI/CD pipeline and verifies that it executes successfully on the staging instance.

blocks.content: Steady-State Governance

After onboarding, the team enters steady-state governance: quarterly RBAC reviews verify that team membership and permissions are current, quarterly ownership registry reviews verify that all DAGs have current owners and up-to-date runbooks, monthly cost reports show the team's resource consumption against their budget, and the weekly scheduling review (Module 3, lesson 10) includes the team's DAGs in the priority and capacity analysis. Steady-state governance is the platform team's primary ongoing interaction with each tenant — the onboarding is a one-time event, but the quarterly reviews recur for the lifetime of the team's tenancy.
The Offboarding Process

When a team is dissolved (the Estonian analytics team was merged into the alliance analytics team during BalticSolar's organizational restructuring), the offboarding process ensures that no resources are orphaned. The process follows a 90-day timeline: Day 1–30, the outgoing team transfers DAG ownership to the receiving team by updating the ownership registry, and the receiving team reviews and acknowledges each transferred DAG. Day 31–60, the platform team migrates RBAC assignments, connection ownership, and cost allocation entries to the receiving team, and verifies that the transferred DAGs continue to execute successfully under the new ownership. Day 61–90, the platform team decommissions the outgoing team's namespace, revokes their RBAC roles, removes their Vault policy entries, and archives their audit history. After 90 days, the outgoing team's resources no longer exist on the platform.

blocks.title: Onboarding speed determines platform adoption
blocks.content: The fastest way to lose a new team's confidence is to make onboarding take 3 weeks of manual setup, platform team coordination, and back-and-forth Slack messages. BalticSolar's target is 1 business day from onboarding request to first DAG deployment on staging. The structured checklist enables this: each step is automated or documented, and the platform team can execute the entire checklist in 2–3 hours. The onboarding speed is a leading indicator of platform health — if onboarding slows down, it means the manual steps have accumulated faster than the automation.

Tenant isolation prevents teams from interfering with each other, but enterprise orchestration requires teams to collaborate: the alliance Gold aggregation DAG depends on all three countries' Silver data, the regulatory filing DAGs consume data produced by the data engineering teams, and the ML model training DAGs access data owned by multiple country teams. Cross-tenant coordination must enable these collaborations without weakening the isolation model. The patterns in this lesson define how teams share DAGs, data, and dependencies while maintaining the RBAC, secrets, and residency controls from the preceding lessons.
Pattern 1: Cross-Tenant Dataset Dependencies

The most common cross-tenant interaction is data dependency: one team's DAG produces a Dataset that another team's DAG consumes. Within a single Airflow instance, the Dataset mechanism handles this transparently — the producing DAG marks the Dataset as updated, and the consuming DAG triggers. The governance question is authorization: should any DAG be allowed to consume any Dataset, or should consumption require explicit subscription? BalticSolar requires explicit subscription: the consuming team must register their Dataset dependency in the ownership registry, and the producing team must acknowledge the subscription. This prevents the scenario where a team unknowingly depends on another team's Dataset and is surprised when the producing team changes the Dataset's schema or schedule.
Pattern 2: Shared DAGs with Dual Ownership

Some DAGs serve two teams equally: the Lithuanian billing reconciliation DAG is owned by both the Lithuanian DE team (who maintains the pipeline code) and the Lithuanian analytics team (who consumes the output and defines the business logic). Dual ownership is implemented through the access_control parameter with both teams receiving edit access, and the ownership registry recording both teams as co-owners with distinct responsibilities. The governance rule: when dual-owned DAGs need modification, both teams must approve the change. The platform team's CI pipeline enforces this by requiring approval from a CODEOWNERS entry for each co-owning team. Dual ownership is used sparingly — only 8 of BalticSolar's 200+ DAGs have dual ownership — because the coordination overhead of two-team approval slows down the change cadence.
Pattern 3: Cross-Country Coordination via the Coordinator

Cross-country DAG dependencies are mediated exclusively through the coordinator instance. A Lithuanian DAG cannot directly trigger or depend on a Latvian DAG because the two run on separate Airflow instances with no shared scheduler. The coordinator instance (Module 1, lesson 2) hosts the alliance-wide DAGs that bridge country boundaries: the Gold aggregation DAGs, the cross-border data exchange DAGs, and the alliance-wide monitoring DAGs. Cross-country coordination follows the REST API trigger pattern from Module 1 (lesson 5), where the coordinator triggers country DAGs via the API with metadata that identifies the requesting context. The key governance rule: cross-country data exchange DAGs can only access aggregated country data, never raw meter data or individual customer records, to maintain data residency compliance.
The Subscription Registry

# balticsolar-subscription-registry.yaml
# Records explicit cross-tenant Dataset dependencies.
# The producing team must acknowledge each subscription.

subscriptions:
  - dataset: "s3://balticsolar-lt/silver/meters/"
    producer:
      team: lt-data-engineering
      dag_id: lt_de_meters_silver
    consumers:
      - team: lt-analytics
        dag_id: lt_analytics_billing_gold
        acknowledged: true
        acknowledged_date: "2025-11-15"
      - team: platform-engineering
        dag_id: eu_platform_alliance_gold
        acknowledged: true
        acknowledged_date: "2025-10-01"
        note: "Cross-instance dependency — coordinator triggers via REST API"
      - team: lt-ml
        dag_id: lt_ml_forecast_gold
        acknowledged: true
        acknowledged_date: "2025-12-01"

  - dataset: "s3://balticsolar-lt/gold/billing/"
    producer:
      team: lt-analytics
      dag_id: lt_analytics_billing_gold
    consumers:
      - team: lt-regulatory
        dag_id: lt_regulatory_vert_filing
        acknowledged: true
        acknowledged_date: "2026-01-15"
      - team: platform-engineering
        dag_id: eu_platform_alliance_gold
        acknowledged: true
        acknowledged_date: "2026-01-15"

The subscription registry makes every cross-tenant data dependency explicit and auditable. When the Lithuanian DE team needs to modify the Silver meters pipeline in a way that could affect the output schema, the registry shows exactly which teams and DAGs depend on the output: the Lithuanian analytics team's billing DAG, the platform team's alliance Gold DAG, and the Lithuanian ML team's forecasting DAG. The modification process requires notifying all three consuming teams, coordinating the change through the schema contract process (analogous to Module 5 of the PySpark senior track), and verifying that all consumers' tests pass against the new schema before the change reaches production.

blocks.title: Explicit subscriptions prevent invisible coupling
blocks.content: Without a subscription registry, cross-tenant dependencies are invisible — a team discovers that another team depends on their Dataset only when they break it. The subscription registry converts these invisible dependencies into explicit, governed relationships. The governance cost is minimal (a YAML entry per subscription, acknowledged by both parties) and the benefit is substantial (every dependency is documented, every change impact is assessable, and no team is surprised by a breaking upstream change).

Multi-tenant governance introduces organizational overhead that is only justified when the isolation, compliance, and resource-sharing benefits exceed the governance cost. Not every organization needs multi-tenancy, and some organizations that have implemented it would be better served by simpler alternatives. This lesson states the boundary conditions where multi-tenant governance creates more operational burden than the problems it solves, and provides the decision framework for determining whether a platform should be multi-tenant, single-tenant, or somewhere in between. The framework is based on BalticSolar's experience evaluating the governance cost against the regulatory and operational constraints that justify it.
The Boundary Conditions

rows: RBAC roles, access control, subscription registry, quarterly reviews — all for 3 teams that could coordinate informally
Single instance with informal conventions. Add RBAC if/when team count reaches 4+.
When team count grows past 3 or a compliance requirement appears
Per-country instances, network isolation, compliance auditing — all for a legal requirement that does not exist
Single instance with RBAC for team isolation. Physical isolation is not needed.
When expanding to a regulated market or when customer contracts require data isolation
Quarterly reviews, compliance reports, tenant lifecycle management — consuming a solo platform engineer's entire capacity
Self-service model with minimal governance. Teams operate independently.
When hiring the second platform engineer
Teams with fundamentally incompatible Airflow versions
Shared instance forces all teams to use the same Airflow version. Version management adds complexity.
Separate instances per version requirement. Let each team manage their own.
When the teams' version requirements converge (e.g., after a platform-wide upgrade)
Factory governance, namespace enforcement, CI validation — overhead exceeds the benefit of standardization
Direct DAG authoring with code review. Add governance as DAG count grows.
When DAG count approaches 50 or cross-team conventions diverge noticeably

blocks.content: The Governance Cost Model

BalticSolar's multi-tenant governance consumes approximately 25% of the platform team's capacity: quarterly RBAC reviews (2 days), quarterly compliance reviews (3 days), quarterly residency audits (1 day), tenant onboarding/offboarding (variable, averaging 2 days/quarter), subscription registry management (1 day/quarter), and ongoing governance tooling maintenance (2 days/quarter). This totals approximately 44 engineering days per year — 11 days per team per year for 4 primary tenant groups (3 countries + coordinator). The governance is justified because the regulatory penalty for a data residency violation exceeds the annual governance cost by orders of magnitude, and the operational benefit of consistent naming, access control, and monitoring standards prevents the ad-hoc practices that caused the incidents described in earlier lessons.
The Migration Path Away from Multi-Tenancy

If the governance cost exceeds the benefit — for example, if BalticSolar's regulatory environment changed to allow shared infrastructure across Baltic states — the migration from multi-tenant to single-tenant follows a reverse of the onboarding process. Country instances are consolidated into a shared instance: DAGs are migrated, connections are merged into a unified secrets path, RBAC is simplified to team-level roles without country-level separation, and the compliance framework is updated to reflect the new regulatory posture. The migration is staged: the coordinator instance absorbs one country's DAGs at a time, with parallel operation during the migration window. The reverse migration (from single-tenant to multi-tenant) follows the topology design process from Module 1 and is significantly more complex because it requires splitting a shared database, redistributing connections, and establishing network isolation boundaries.

blocks.title: Multi-tenancy is justified by constraints, not by ambition
blocks.content: The urge to implement enterprise-grade multi-tenancy on a platform with 2 teams and 15 DAGs is a form of premature optimization. The governance overhead consumes engineering time that should be spent building data pipelines, not managing RBAC roles and compliance reports. BalticSolar's multi-tenancy is justified by three non-negotiable constraints: regulatory data residency (cannot be waived), 10 teams with competing priorities (informal coordination does not scale), and cross-country data exchange requirements (require formal coordination). If any of these constraints disappeared, the governance model would be simplified accordingly.

This lesson synthesizes the entire module into BalticSolar Alliance's multi-tenant governance framework. Each preceding lesson addressed one dimension of governance: the tenancy model (lesson 1), RBAC design (lesson 2), DAG-level access control (lesson 3), data residency isolation (lesson 4), secrets management (lesson 5), compliance framework (lesson 6), tenant lifecycle (lesson 7), cross-tenant coordination (lesson 8), and governance boundaries (lesson 9). This lesson traces how these dimensions compose into a complete governance framework and how the organizational processes maintain governance integrity as the platform evolves through team changes, regulatory updates, and capacity growth.
The Governance Framework Summary

rows: Hybrid: per-country instances + RBAC within instances
Custom roles per team with code-managed definitions
Deployment applies RBAC; CI validates hand-crafted DAG access_control
CI pipeline rejects DAGs without conforming access_control
HashiCorp Vault with per-country secret engines
Vault policies enforce country-level access boundaries
Audit export pipeline + compliance warehouse + quarterly review
Checklist tracked in project management + platform team execution
CI validates subscription acknowledgments + coordinator API

blocks.content: How Governance Connects to Previous Modules

The governance framework depends on every preceding module's architecture. The platform topology (Module 1) provides the per-country instances that enable physical isolation. The DAG factory (Module 2) automates access_control assignment and namespace enforcement, eliminating the governance burden of manually configuring 180 DAGs. The scheduling architecture (Module 3) provides the pool and priority system that prevents cross-tenant resource starvation. The executor architecture (Module 4) provides the per-country executor configuration that keeps task execution within country boundaries. The integration architecture (Module 5) provides the connection contracts that formalize cross-system access. The scheduler internals (Module 6) provide the HA and state machine knowledge needed to diagnose governance violations that manifest as scheduling anomalies. The metadata database (Module 7) provides the audit trail infrastructure and the secrets backend that the governance framework enforces.
The Quarterly Governance Committee

Every quarter, the governance committee convenes with representatives from the platform team, the compliance team, and each country's team lead. The committee reviews five governance dimensions: RBAC compliance (has any unauthorized access been detected?), residency compliance (have any cross-boundary violations been detected?), audit completeness (is the compliance data warehouse receiving all required events?), tenant health (are all teams current on ownership reviews and runbook maintenance?), and cross-tenant coordination (are subscription registrations current and acknowledged?). The committee produces an action list that feeds into the next quarter's platform engineering roadmap, and the compliance team uses the committee's findings for regulatory filings.
Looking Forward: Observability and Enterprise Governance

Module 9 extends the governance framework into operational territory: how incidents are detected, classified, and resolved across the multi-tenant platform. The observability architecture (Module 9) builds on the RBAC and access control from this module to ensure that alerts are routed to the correct tenant's on-call engineer. Module 10 ties the entire track together with enterprise governance processes: capacity planning that accounts for multi-tenant resource sharing, version upgrade strategies that respect per-tenant stability requirements, and the organizational structures that sustain the governance framework over years.

blocks.title: Governance is the platform's immune system
blocks.content: Technical controls (RBAC, network isolation, Vault policies) prevent governance violations in real time. Audit mechanisms (the compliance warehouse, the residency checker, the RBAC audit) detect violations that slipped through the technical controls. Organizational processes (the quarterly review, the subscription registry, the onboarding checklist) prevent the conditions that lead to violations. Together, these three mechanisms form the platform's immune system: prevention, detection, and adaptation. A governance framework that has only technical controls but no audit or process layer catches current violations but cannot detect novel ones. A framework with all three layers improves over time because each quarterly review identifies gaps that the next quarter's technical controls address.

chapters.title: Advanced Observability and Incident Architecture
chapters.description: Design enterprise observability and incident management for a multi-instance orchestration platform — cross-instance monitoring, SLA governance across countries, alerting architecture, incident detection and response, chaos engineering, and capacity forecasting.

blocks.content: The mid-level track built observability around individual DAG monitoring: task duration charts, success/failure counts, and the Airflow UI's built-in DAG grid view. These per-DAG metrics are necessary but insufficient at BalticSolar scale. When 200+ DAGs run across 4 Airflow instances, per-DAG monitoring produces 200+ individual signals that no single engineer can watch simultaneously. The senior-level shift is from monitoring individual DAGs to observing the platform as a system: detecting patterns that span multiple DAGs, correlating failures across instances, and identifying systemic issues (scheduler degradation, database contention, worker fleet exhaustion) that manifest as DAG failures but originate in the platform infrastructure. Platform-level observability answers the question 'is the orchestration platform healthy?' rather than 'did this specific DAG succeed?'
The Three Observability Layers

rows: Scheduler, executor, metadata database, worker fleet across all instances
Heartbeat duration, scheduling delay, DB query latency P95, worker utilization, DAG parsing time
Platform team — daily morning review + automated alerts
Individual DAG success rates, task durations, SLA achievement per DAG
DAG success rate (7-day rolling), task P95 duration, SLA on-time percentage, data freshness
Team on-call — per-team dashboards + team-routed alerts
Data availability for downstream consumers, regulatory filing readiness, alliance-wide aggregation completeness
Gold table freshness, regulatory filing completion, alliance Gold SLA, consumer team satisfaction
Alliance leadership — weekly summary + monthly review

blocks.content: The Observability Stack

blocks.title: BalticSolar observability architecture
blocks.caption: All four Airflow instances emit StatsD metrics to a central Prometheus. Grafana provides the three dashboard layers. PagerDuty handles P1/P2 alerts. Slack delivers P3 and informational notifications.

visual-recommendation.description: Architecture diagram showing four Airflow instances pushing metrics to central Prometheus, with Grafana (three dashboard layers), PagerDuty (critical alerts), and Slack (informational) as consumers. Include metric flow annotations and alert routing paths.

blocks.content: Airflow emits metrics via StatsD — a lightweight UDP-based metrics protocol. Each Airflow component (scheduler, web server, workers) emits metrics tagged with the component type and the instance name. A StatsD exporter on each instance translates these metrics into Prometheus format, and a central Prometheus server scrapes all four exporters every 15 seconds. Prometheus stores the time-series data with instance labels (lt, lv, ee, coordinator), enabling both per-instance queries ('what is the Lithuanian scheduler's heartbeat duration?') and cross-instance aggregations ('what is the alliance-wide DAG success rate?'). Grafana connects to Prometheus and renders the three dashboard layers. PagerDuty receives critical alerts from Prometheus Alertmanager. Slack receives informational notifications via webhook.
Why Per-DAG Monitoring Is Insufficient

Per-DAG monitoring answers 'did my DAG succeed?' but cannot answer 'why did 15 DAGs fail simultaneously?' or 'is the scheduler degrading before any DAG fails?' At BalticSolar, the most operationally expensive incidents are systemic: a metadata database slowdown that increases scheduling delay for all 80 Lithuanian DAGs, a worker fleet OOM event that kills 12 concurrent tasks across 8 teams, or a network partition between the coordinator and the Estonian instance that silently prevents cross-country triggering. These systemic issues are invisible to per-DAG monitoring because each individual DAG failure looks like an unrelated incident. Platform-level observability correlates the individual failures into a single systemic pattern and alerts the platform team before the per-DAG alert storm overwhelms the on-call engineers.

blocks.title: Observability is correlation, not collection
blocks.content: Every Airflow instance already collects metrics — StatsD emissions, task logs, scheduler logs, executor logs. The challenge at 200+ DAGs is not collecting more data but correlating the data that already exists. A scheduling delay on the Lithuanian instance, a database latency spike on the same instance, and 15 DAG failures within a 2-minute window are three separate signals that together indicate a single root cause (database contention). The platform dashboard's value is making this correlation visible within 30 seconds rather than requiring an engineer to check three separate log sources and mentally reconstruct the timeline over 30 minutes.

The mid-level track defined SLAs as per-DAG timeout parameters: SaulėGrid's Bronze DAG had an SLA of 90 minutes, and a missed SLA triggered a Slack notification. At BalticSolar scale, SLAs are not individual DAG parameters — they are a governance framework that determines which DAGs are critical, what 'on time' means for each criticality tier, how SLA achievement is measured and reported, and what organizational response a missed SLA triggers. The SLA framework connects scheduling priority (Module 3), resource allocation (Module 4), and compliance reporting (Module 8) into a unified system where every DAG's importance is classified, every classification has a measurable target, and every target has a governance process for review and adjustment.
The SLA Tier Framework

rows: P1 alert → immediate on-call response → post-mortem required
VERT regulatory filing, Bronze meter ingestion (regulatory chain)
P2 alert → response within 1 hour → incident ticket
Billing settlement, alliance Gold aggregation, Silver transforms
P3 notification → team reviews in next standup
Analytics reports, market analysis, data quality dashboards
No alert — failure logged for weekly review
Experimental DAGs, ad-hoc analysis, backfill operations

blocks.content: SLA Achievement Calculation

"""BalticSolar SLA achievement calculator.

Runs daily on the coordinator instance to calculate per-DAG
and per-tier SLA achievement across all country instances.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.providers.postgres.hooks.postgres import PostgresHook

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class SLAResult:
    dag_id: str
    tier: str
    country: str
    period_start: datetime
    period_end: datetime
    total_runs: int
    on_time_runs: int
    achievement_pct: float
    target_pct: float
    meets_target: bool


@task
def calculate_sla_achievement(
    country: str,
    period_days: int = 30,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Calculate SLA achievement for all DAGs on a country instance.

    A DagRun is 'on time' if it completed within the DAG's
    sla_minutes target. The achievement percentage is the
    ratio of on-time runs to total runs in the measurement window.
    """
    hook = PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id=f'{country}_airflow_metadata')
    period_start = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=period_days)

    # Query DagRun completion times and SLA targets
    results = hook.get_records(f"""
        SELECT
            dr.dag_id,
            count(*) AS total_runs,
            count(*) FILTER (
                WHERE EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (dr.end_date - dr.start_date)) / 60
                      <= COALESCE(d.default_args::json->>'sla_minutes', '60')::float
            ) AS on_time_runs
        FROM dag_run dr
        JOIN dag d ON dr.dag_id = d.dag_id
        WHERE dr.state = 'success'
            AND dr.end_date >= '{period_start.isoformat()}'
            AND dr.run_type = 'scheduled'
        GROUP BY dr.dag_id
        ORDER BY dr.dag_id
    """)

    # Load SLA tier assignments from the ownership registry
    from balticsolar_common.registry import load_ownership_registry
    registry = load_ownership_registry()

    sla_results = []
    for dag_id, total_runs, on_time_runs in results:
        entry = registry.get(dag_id, {})
        tier = entry.get('sla_tier', 'bronze')
        target = {'platinum': 99.9, 'gold': 99.5, 'silver': 95.0, 'bronze': 0}.get(tier, 0)

        achievement = (on_time_runs / total_runs * 100) if total_runs > 0 else 100.0

        sla_results.append(SLAResult(
            dag_id=dag_id,
            tier=tier,
            country=country,
            period_start=period_start,
            period_end=datetime.utcnow(),
            total_runs=total_runs,
            on_time_runs=on_time_runs,
            achievement_pct=round(achievement, 2),
            target_pct=target,
            meets_target=achievement >= target,
        ).__dict__)

        if not (achievement >= target) and tier in ('platinum', 'gold'):
            logger.warning(
                "SLA MISS: %s (%s tier) — %.1f%% vs %.1f%% target",
                dag_id, tier, achievement, target,
            )

    return sla_results

The SLA calculation uses the DagRun's actual duration (end_date minus start_date) compared against the DAG's sla_minutes parameter. A DagRun is 'on time' if its duration is within the SLA budget. The achievement percentage is the ratio of on-time runs to total scheduled runs in the measurement window (30-day rolling). The calculation excludes manually triggered runs (run_type='manual') because ad-hoc triggers are not SLA-governed, and excludes failed runs that were retried and eventually succeeded (only the final successful run counts). The SLA results feed into three outputs: the compliance report (Module 8, lesson 6), the scheduling priority review (Module 3, lesson 10), and the monthly SLA review where the governance committee evaluates whether tier assignments and targets need adjustment.
Cross-Country SLA Coordination

BalticSolar's alliance-wide Gold DAGs have SLAs that depend on all three country Silver pipelines completing on time. The alliance Gold DAG's SLA is gold tier (99.5% on-time, complete by 06:00 CET), but it can only achieve this if the Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian Silver DAGs complete by 04:00 EET — leaving 2 hours of buffer for the coordinator's aggregation. This cascading SLA requirement means that the country Silver DAGs' effective SLA target is determined by the alliance Gold DAG's deadline, not by their own independent schedule. The SLA framework models these cascading dependencies: each DAG's SLA registry entry includes an upstream_sla_dependency field that references the downstream DAG whose SLA constrains the upstream's effective deadline. The monthly SLA review evaluates the cascade chain and adjusts country-level targets when the alliance-level target changes.

blocks.title: SLAs are contracts, not aspirations
blocks.content: An SLA tier assignment is not a label — it is a resource allocation decision. Platinum DAGs get dedicated pool slots (Module 3), highest priority weight, dedicated alerting channels, and mandatory post-mortems for every miss. Gold DAGs get reserved capacity and P2 alerting. Silver DAGs get shared resources and P3 notifications. Assigning a DAG to a higher tier increases its resource allocation at the expense of other DAGs. This is why tier assignment is governed by the quarterly review rather than self-service: if every team self-assigns platinum, the resource reservation system collapses (Module 3, lesson 9 — priority inflation anti-pattern).

At SaulėGrid's 12-DAG scale, every DAG failure generated a Slack notification to the team channel, and the team's on-call engineer reviewed each notification manually. At BalticSolar's 200+ DAGs, this approach produces 5–15 failure notifications per day across the alliance — enough to create alert fatigue where the on-call engineer stops reading notifications because most are low-severity silver-tier DAGs that can wait until the morning standup. Alert fatigue is the most dangerous observability failure because it does not manifest as a broken system — it manifests as a platinum-tier SLA breach that went unnoticed for 45 minutes because the on-call engineer had muted the notification channel after the tenth silver-tier alert that hour.
Alert Routing by Severity

rows: Platinum DAG SLA breach, scheduler failure, metadata DB unreachable, worker fleet < 50% capacity
PagerDuty page to on-call engineer + phone call if unacknowledged in 5 min
5 min → on-call, 15 min → country lead, 30 min → platform lead, 60 min → alliance CTO
Gold DAG SLA breach, cross-instance coordination failure, executor backend unhealthy
1 hour → country lead, 4 hours → platform lead
Silver DAG failure, scheduling delay > 30 sec, data freshness warning
Reviewed in weekly scheduling review if recurring
DAG completion, maintenance task success, capacity metric update
Logged to observability dashboard only — no notification

blocks.content: Alert Deduplication and Root Cause Correlation

"""BalticSolar alert deduplication and correlation.

Prevents a single root cause from generating 50 individual
DAG failure alerts. Groups related alerts into a single
incident and routes the incident to the appropriate team.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

CORRELATION_WINDOW = timedelta(minutes=5)


@dataclass
class AlertEvent:
    dag_id: str
    instance: str
    event_type: str             # task_failed, sla_miss, scheduler_slow
    timestamp: datetime
    severity: str               # P1, P2, P3, P4
    details: dict = field(default_factory=dict)


@dataclass
class CorrelatedIncident:
    incident_id: str
    root_cause_hypothesis: str
    severity: str               # highest severity among correlated alerts
    instance: str
    alerts: list[AlertEvent]
    created_at: datetime
    assigned_team: str


def correlate_alerts(
    new_alert: AlertEvent,
    recent_alerts: list[AlertEvent],
) -> CorrelatedIncident | None:
    """Attempt to correlate a new alert with recent alerts.

    Correlation rules:
    1. Same instance + same 5-minute window → likely same root cause
    2. 3+ DAG failures on same instance in 5 min → systemic issue
    3. Scheduler slow + DAG failures → scheduler is the root cause
    4. DB latency spike + scheduler slow → DB is the root cause
    """
    window_start = new_alert.timestamp - CORRELATION_WINDOW
    related = [
        a for a in recent_alerts
        if a.instance == new_alert.instance
        and a.timestamp >= window_start
    ]

    if not related:
        return None

    # Rule 3: scheduler issue is likely root cause
    scheduler_alerts = [a for a in related if a.event_type == 'scheduler_slow']
    if scheduler_alerts and new_alert.event_type == 'task_failed':
        return CorrelatedIncident(
            incident_id=f'INC-{new_alert.timestamp.strftime("%Y%m%d%H%M")}',
            root_cause_hypothesis='Scheduler degradation causing cascading task failures',
            severity='P1',
            instance=new_alert.instance,
            alerts=related + [new_alert],
            created_at=new_alert.timestamp,
            assigned_team='platform-engineering',
        )

    # Rule 4: DB latency is likely root cause
    db_alerts = [a for a in related if 'db_latency' in a.event_type]
    if db_alerts and new_alert.event_type in ('scheduler_slow', 'task_failed'):
        return CorrelatedIncident(
            incident_id=f'INC-{new_alert.timestamp.strftime("%Y%m%d%H%M")}',
            root_cause_hypothesis='Metadata database latency causing scheduling degradation',
            severity='P1',
            instance=new_alert.instance,
            alerts=related + [new_alert],
            created_at=new_alert.timestamp,
            assigned_team='platform-engineering',
        )

    # Rule 2: 3+ failures in window → systemic
    failure_alerts = [a for a in related if a.event_type == 'task_failed']
    if len(failure_alerts) >= 3:
        teams = {a.details.get('owner_team', 'unknown') for a in failure_alerts}
        return CorrelatedIncident(
            incident_id=f'INC-{new_alert.timestamp.strftime("%Y%m%d%H%M")}',
            root_cause_hypothesis=f'Systemic failure affecting {len(failure_alerts)+1} DAGs across {len(teams)} teams',
            severity='P2' if len(failure_alerts) < 5 else 'P1',
            instance=new_alert.instance,
            alerts=failure_alerts + [new_alert],
            created_at=new_alert.timestamp,
            assigned_team='platform-engineering',
        )

    return None

The correlation engine groups related alerts into a single incident before any notification is sent. Without correlation, a metadata database slowdown that causes 15 DAGs to fail generates 15 individual P2/P3 alerts to 8 different teams — each team investigates independently, consumes 30 minutes diagnosing their own DAG failure, and eventually discovers that the root cause is a platform issue that only the platform team can fix. With correlation, the same 15 failures generate one P1 incident assigned to the platform team with the hypothesis 'metadata database latency causing scheduling degradation,' and the team-level alerts are suppressed until the platform incident is resolved. This reduces the total investigation time from 4+ hours (8 teams × 30 minutes) to 30 minutes (1 platform team investigation).
Team-Based Alert Routing

Even after deduplication and correlation, alerts must reach the correct team. BalticSolar's routing uses the namespace convention (Module 1, lesson 3) as the routing key: an alert for lt_de_meters_bronze is routed to the #lt-de-oncall Slack channel and the Lithuanian DE team's PagerDuty on-call schedule. An alert for eu_platform_alliance_gold is routed to the platform team's PagerDuty schedule. The routing configuration is derived from the ownership registry (Module 1, lesson 3) — the same YAML file that maps DAGs to teams for incident routing, cost allocation, and governance. A single source of truth for DAG ownership ensures that alerts reach the team that can actually investigate and fix the issue, not a generic platform channel where the alert competes with 50 other notifications.

blocks.title: Alert fatigue kills faster than system failures
blocks.content: A platform that generates 50 alerts per day will eventually have every non-P1 alert ignored. The on-call engineer learns to dismiss the notifications because 90% are silver-tier failures that resolve on retry. The 91st notification — a gold-tier SLA breach that requires immediate action — is dismissed along with the rest. BalticSolar's alert hygiene policy (lesson 9) addresses this by requiring that every alert has a documented response action. Alerts without a response action are informational noise and should be dashboard metrics, not notifications.

The mid-level track detected incidents through task failure alerts: a task fails, Airflow sends a notification, the on-call engineer investigates. At BalticSolar scale, some of the most damaging incidents are not task failures — they are slow degradations that erode SLA margins without triggering any individual failure alert. A Silver DAG that normally takes 20 minutes gradually increases to 35 minutes over two weeks. No individual run fails, but the SLA buffer shrinks from 40 minutes to 5 minutes, and the next external delay (a slow SFTP transfer, a database maintenance window) pushes the DAG past its SLA. Senior-level incident detection identifies these degradation patterns before they cause SLA breaches, using anomaly detection on execution metrics rather than binary success/failure monitoring.
Detection Patterns Beyond Task Failure

rows: A DAG taking significantly longer than its historical baseline
Rolling P95 duration exceeds 2× the 30-day baseline
P3 (warning) → P2 if sustained for 3+ days
A DAG's weekly success rate declining gradually
7-day rolling success rate drops below tier threshold minus 5%
A Gold table is stale beyond its SLA window
Freshness check DAG queries table metadata for last_updated timestamp
P1 (platinum) or P2 (gold) based on table's SLA tier
3+ downstream DAGs failing because of one upstream failure
Correlation engine (lesson 3) detects clustered failures with common upstream dependency
P1 (if platinum DAGs in cascade) or P2
DAGs consistently starting 5+ minutes later than scheduled over a week
Actual start time vs scheduled start time, trending over 7 days
P3 (warning) — may indicate scheduler capacity pressure
A DAG that normally retries 0–1 times suddenly retries 3 times per run
P3 — often indicates an external system degradation

blocks.content: The Duration Anomaly Detector

"""Duration anomaly detection for BalticSolar DAGs.

Compares each DAG's recent execution duration against its
historical baseline and flags DAGs that are trending slower.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.providers.postgres.hooks.postgres import PostgresHook

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

ANOMALY_THRESHOLD = 2.0  # flag if P95 > 2× baseline


@dag(
    dag_id='platform_duration_anomaly_detector',
    schedule='@hourly',
    catchup=False,
    tags=['platform', 'monitoring', 'anomaly'],
)
def duration_anomaly_detector():

    @task
    def detect_anomalies(country: str) -> list[dict]:
        """Compare recent P95 duration against 30-day baseline."""
        hook = PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id=f'{country}_airflow_metadata')

        anomalies = hook.get_records(f"""
            WITH baseline AS (
                SELECT
                    dag_id,
                    percentile_cont(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (
                        ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (end_date - start_date))
                    ) AS p95_baseline_sec
                FROM dag_run
                WHERE state = 'success'
                    AND run_type = 'scheduled'
                    AND end_date >= now() - interval '30 days'
                    AND end_date < now() - interval '3 days'
                GROUP BY dag_id
                HAVING count(*) >= 10
            ),
            recent AS (
                SELECT
                    dag_id,
                    percentile_cont(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (
                        ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (end_date - start_date))
                    ) AS p95_recent_sec
                FROM dag_run
                WHERE state = 'success'
                    AND run_type = 'scheduled'
                    AND end_date >= now() - interval '3 days'
                GROUP BY dag_id
                HAVING count(*) >= 3
            )
            SELECT
                b.dag_id,
                round(b.p95_baseline_sec::numeric, 1) AS baseline_sec,
                round(r.p95_recent_sec::numeric, 1) AS recent_sec,
                round((r.p95_recent_sec / NULLIF(b.p95_baseline_sec, 0))::numeric, 2) AS ratio
            FROM baseline b
            JOIN recent r ON b.dag_id = r.dag_id
            WHERE r.p95_recent_sec > b.p95_baseline_sec * {ANOMALY_THRESHOLD}
            ORDER BY ratio DESC
        """)

        results = []
        for dag_id, baseline, recent, ratio in anomalies:
            logger.warning(
                "DURATION ANOMALY: %s — recent P95 %.0fs vs baseline %.0fs (%.1f×)",
                dag_id, recent, baseline, ratio,
            )
            results.append({
                'dag_id': dag_id,
                'baseline_p95_sec': float(baseline),
                'recent_p95_sec': float(recent),
                'ratio': float(ratio),
                'country': country,
            })

        return results

    detect_anomalies(country='lt')

duration_anomaly_detector()

The anomaly detector compares each DAG's recent 3-day P95 duration against its 30-day historical baseline. A DAG whose recent P95 exceeds 2× the baseline is flagged as anomalous. The detection runs hourly, catching degradation within hours rather than waiting for an SLA breach. The baseline excludes the most recent 3 days to prevent the anomaly itself from inflating the baseline — a common statistical error in anomaly detection where a sustained degradation gradually becomes the 'new normal' and stops triggering alerts. The 10-run minimum for baseline and 3-run minimum for recent data prevent false positives on DAGs with irregular schedules.
Severity Classification

Detected incidents are classified by severity based on two factors: the SLA tier of the affected DAGs and the number of downstream consumers impacted. A duration anomaly on a platinum DAG is P2 regardless of the anomaly magnitude, because any degradation of a platinum DAG threatens a regulatory deadline. A cascade failure affecting 5+ DAGs is P1 regardless of the individual DAGs' tiers, because the blast radius indicates a systemic issue. The classification matrix is encoded in the alerting rules and updated quarterly during the SLA review — when a DAG's tier changes (Module 9, lesson 2), its incident classification thresholds change automatically.

blocks.title: The best incident detection catches problems before they become incidents
blocks.content: A task failure is an incident — it has already happened and requires reactive response. A duration anomaly is a pre-incident signal — the DAG has not failed yet, but it is trending toward failure. A scheduling drift pattern is an early warning — the platform's scheduling capacity is eroding before any DAG is directly affected. The detection patterns in this lesson progress from reactive (task failure) through proactive (anomaly detection) to predictive (trend analysis). The further upstream the detection, the more time the on-call engineer has to investigate and remediate before the issue escalates to a customer-visible SLA breach.

When an incident is detected and classified, the on-call engineer's first action should not be 'start investigating from scratch.' It should be 'open the playbook for this incident type and follow the documented steps.' A playbook — also called a runbook — codifies the diagnosis steps, recovery procedures, communication protocols, and escalation criteria for each incident type. At BalticSolar scale with 200+ DAGs, 15+ external system integrations, and 4 Airflow instances, no single engineer can hold the complete diagnosis tree in their head. Playbooks convert institutional knowledge into repeatable procedures that any qualified on-call engineer can follow, regardless of whether they have personally encountered the specific incident type before.
The Playbook Hierarchy

rows: Diagnosis and recovery for a specific DAG failure
lt_de_meters_bronze fails: check SFTP connection, verify file availability, check schema version, retry or escalate
Diagnosis and recovery for external system failures
Nord Pool API unavailable: verify API status page, check rate limit, switch to fallback data source, notify affected DAGs
Diagnosis and recovery for infrastructure issues
Scheduler HA failover: verify surviving scheduler health, check metadata DB connectivity, monitor DagRun creation continuity, restart failed process
Coordination when an incident on one instance affects others
Lithuanian Silver delay affects coordinator Gold: notify coordinator on-call, adjust Gold SLA expectation, communicate to downstream consumers

blocks.content: The Playbook Template

# balticsolar-runbooks/dags/lt_de_meters_bronze.yaml
# Runbook for the Lithuanian Bronze meter ingestion DAG.

dag_id: lt_de_meters_bronze
owner_team: lt-data-engineering
contact: "#lt-de-oncall"
sla_tier: platinum
last_updated: "2026-03-15"
reviewed_by: "Jonas K., LT-DE lead"

common_failure_modes:
  - name: SFTP connection timeout
    symptoms:
      - "Task 'ingest_meters' failed with ConnectionError"
      - "Retry 1/3 in progress, same error"
    diagnosis:
      - "Check SFTP server status: ssh lt-sftp-meters.saulegrid.lt"
      - "Verify credentials in Vault: vault kv get secret/balticsolar/lt/sftp_meters"
      - "Check network connectivity from worker to SFTP: nc -zv lt-sftp-meters.saulegrid.lt 22"
    recovery:
      - "If SFTP server is down: wait for infrastructure team to restore. ETA from #lt-infra."
      - "If credentials expired: rotate in Vault (see credential rotation runbook)."
      - "If network issue: check VPC peering status in AWS console."
    escalation: "If not resolved in 30 min → escalate to platform team (#platform-oncall)"

  - name: Schema version mismatch
    symptoms:
      - "Task 'validate_schema' failed with SchemaValidationError"
      - "Expected schema v3, got v4"
    diagnosis:
      - "Check if meter data provider published a schema change notice"
      - "Compare received schema against contract: contracts/lt-sftp-meters.yaml"
    recovery:
      - "If schema change is backward-compatible: update schema version in pipeline config"
      - "If breaking change: coordinate with data provider for rollback or update factory config"
    escalation: "Schema changes affecting platinum DAGs → P1 incident"

  - name: Data volume anomaly
    symptoms:
      - "Quality check 'row_count_check' failed: expected 100K-200K, got 15K"
    diagnosis:
      - "Verify date range in source files: ls /data/exports/meters/{{ ds }}/"
      - "Check if partial upload: compare file count against expected"
    recovery:
      - "If partial upload: wait for complete upload and re-trigger"
      - "If genuinely low volume: verify with data provider (holiday? system outage?)"
    escalation: "If unresolved by 01:30 EET → Silver SLA at risk → notify Silver DAG owner"

manual_intervention:
  clear_and_retry: "airflow dags trigger lt_de_meters_bronze --conf '{\"force_rerun\": true}'"
  skip_and_continue: "Mark task as success in UI → downstream Silver will use previous day's data"
  rollback: "Not applicable — Bronze is append-only, bad data is overwritten by re-run"

The playbook template covers every element an on-call engineer needs during an incident: the failure mode (what went wrong), the symptoms (what the engineer sees in logs and alerts), the diagnosis steps (how to identify the root cause), the recovery procedures (how to fix it), and the escalation criteria (when to involve other teams). The playbook is YAML rather than a wiki page because YAML is version-controlled, reviewable, and machine-parseable — the incident management system can link directly to the relevant playbook section when routing an alert. BalticSolar's CI pipeline enforces the rule that every production DAG must have a corresponding playbook file in the runbooks directory. A DAG without a playbook is rejected by CI.
The Playbook Review Process

Playbooks degrade over time: connection names change, diagnosis commands become outdated, escalation contacts rotate, and new failure modes emerge that the original playbook does not cover. BalticSolar's playbook review process requires each playbook to be reviewed by the owning team within 90 days of its last review. The review verifies that the diagnosis commands work (run each command and confirm it produces useful output), the recovery procedures are current (connection names, API endpoints, and Vault paths are correct), and the escalation contacts are current (the on-call rotation has not changed). Playbooks that are overdue for review are flagged in the governance dashboard (Module 8, lesson 10), and DAGs with overdue playbooks generate a P4 warning during the weekly scheduling review.

blocks.title: A playbook that is never tested is a wish list
blocks.content: BalticSolar's chaos engineering program (lesson 7) deliberately triggers the failure modes documented in playbooks to verify that the diagnosis steps work and the recovery procedures succeed. A playbook that says 'restart the scheduler using systemctl restart airflow-scheduler' is useless if the scheduler is running in a Kubernetes pod where systemctl is not available. Chaos tests catch these discrepancies between the playbook's assumptions and the production environment's reality. Every chaos test updates the tested playbook with corrected commands and timings.

Every P1 incident at BalticSolar requires a blameless post-mortem within 5 business days of resolution. P2 incidents require a post-mortem if they recur within 30 days or if the incident affected multiple teams. The post-mortem process is not a bureaucratic requirement — it is the mechanism by which the platform improves over time. Without post-mortems, the same incident recurs because the contributing factors are never identified and addressed. With post-mortems, each incident produces action items that reduce the probability of recurrence. BalticSolar's post-mortem database tracks 47 incidents from the first 12 months, with 156 action items of which 134 (86%) have been completed. The 22 open items are tracked in the quarterly governance review.
The Post-Mortem Template

rows: Minute-by-minute reconstruction from detection to resolution, including all communication events
On-call engineer who handled the incident
Which DAGs were affected, which SLAs were breached, which downstream consumers were delayed, estimated data freshness impact
The single technical or organizational failure that initiated the incident chain. Not 'the engineer made a mistake' but 'the system allowed the mistake to reach production.'
Platform team (root cause requires systemic analysis)
Other conditions that amplified the impact or delayed detection/resolution. Often includes monitoring gaps, playbook deficiencies, or communication delays.
All participants in the post-mortem discussion
Specific, assignable, deadlined improvements that address the root cause and contributing factors. Each item has an owner and a due date.
Post-mortem participants, approved by platform lead
What worked well (detection speed, communication, recovery), what did not (monitoring gaps, unclear playbook, delayed escalation), and what the team would do differently

blocks.content: The Root Cause Taxonomy

rows: Failure originated outside the platform — external API, data provider, infrastructure service
Nord Pool API returned empty response during scheduled maintenance that was not communicated
Incorrect or outdated configuration on the Airflow instance or a connected system
PgBouncer connection pool size insufficient after worker fleet scale-up
Bug in DAG code, factory code, provider code, or plugin code
Factory template generated DAGs with incorrect SLA parameter after template merge conflict
Resource exhaustion — worker slots, database connections, memory, disk
Lithuanian worker fleet hit 100% slot utilization during midnight thundering herd
Cloud service outage, network partition, DNS failure, certificate expiry
AWS eu-north-1 availability zone degradation affecting Estonian worker nodes
Human error enabled by inadequate process — missing review, skipped test, incomplete playbook
Credential rotation completed in Vault but verification test was skipped — failure detected 6 hours later

blocks.content: The root cause taxonomy enables trend analysis across incidents. BalticSolar's Year 1 distribution shows that external dependencies (32%) and configuration (23%) together cause 55% of incidents — a pattern that drove the integration contract system (Module 5) and the configuration drift detection (Module 1, lesson 6). The code category (17%) dropped from 25% in Q1 to 12% in Q4 as the factory test suite (Module 2, lesson 6) and integration testing (Module 5, lesson 7) matured. The capacity category (13%) is concentrated in the first quarter when the alliance merger's combined workload exceeded the original capacity planning — the capacity forecasting system (lesson 8) was built specifically to prevent recurrence.
Action Item Tracking

Post-mortem action items are tracked in the platform engineering project board alongside regular engineering work. Each action item has an owner (the engineer responsible for implementation), a due date (typically 2–4 weeks), a priority (P1 action items from platinum incident post-mortems take precedence over feature work), and a verification criterion (how the platform team confirms the action item is effective). Action items that are not completed by their due date are escalated to the platform lead in the weekly team standup. The quarterly governance review (Module 8, lesson 10) includes an action item completion report: the percentage of action items completed on time is a leading indicator of platform improvement pace.

blocks.title: Blameless does not mean actionless
blocks.content: A blameless post-mortem does not blame individuals, but it does identify systemic weaknesses and assign action items to fix them. 'The engineer deployed a bad config' is blame. 'The CI pipeline did not validate the config against the schema, allowing a malformed config to reach production' is a systemic finding with an actionable fix (add schema validation to CI). The blameless principle redirects investigation from 'who made the mistake' to 'what system allowed the mistake to reach production' — which always produces more durable improvements because it addresses the root cause rather than the proximate trigger.

Chaos engineering is the practice of deliberately injecting failures into a production or production-like system to test its resilience. At BalticSolar, chaos engineering serves two purposes: verifying that the platform's resilience mechanisms actually work (scheduler HA failover, worker fleet recovery, playbook accuracy), and discovering failure modes that the design did not anticipate. The mid-level track tested resilience through CI/CD pipelines and staging environments — controlled, predictable testing. Senior-level chaos engineering tests resilience under realistic conditions: the failure happens at an unexpected time, the on-call engineer discovers it through the monitoring system rather than the test plan, and the recovery follows the playbook rather than a pre-rehearsed script.
The Chaos Test Catalog

rows: Kill one of two scheduler processes on a country instance
Surviving scheduler continues scheduling within 1 heartbeat; killed process restarts within 30 seconds
Worker fleet resilience and task retry
Set memory limit to 512 MB on one Celery worker (normal: 2 GB) during task execution
Worker is killed by OOM; running tasks are retried on surviving workers; fleet scales replacement
Temporarily rotate a staging connection to invalid credentials
Tasks fail with PermanentIntegrationError (not retried); alert routes to correct team; recovery follows playbook
Add 500ms latency to DB queries using pg_sleep proxy
Scheduling delay increases; P2 alert fires; platform team identifies DB latency as root cause within 5 minutes
Block network traffic between coordinator and one country instance
Coordinator's readiness sensor times out for affected country; P2 alert fires; alliance Gold DAG runs with partial data (quorum pattern from Module 3, lesson 5)

blocks.content: The Chaos Engineering Protocol

Every chaos test follows a strict protocol to prevent test-induced production impact. First, the test scope is defined and approved by the platform lead — which instance, which failure type, which time window. Second, the test is announced to the affected country team's on-call engineer 1 hour before injection — the on-call engineer knows a test is happening but does not know the specific failure type, simulating a realistic detection scenario. Third, the failure is injected during a non-peak window (BalticSolar schedules chaos tests between 10:00 and 14:00 EET, outside the overnight batch window). Fourth, the on-call engineer responds using the normal incident process — monitoring detection, playbook consultation, recovery execution, communication to affected teams. Fifth, the platform team observes the response and records the time to detection, time to diagnosis, time to recovery, and any gaps in the playbook.
Incidents Prevented by Chaos Engineering

In 12 months, BalticSolar's chaos engineering program identified 3 resilience gaps that would have caused production incidents. The first was a scheduler HA configuration error on the Estonian instance: the second scheduler process was configured but never started, so the HA failover test killed the only running scheduler and the instance lost scheduling for 4 minutes. The gap was fixed by adding a startup check that verifies both scheduler processes are running. The second was a playbook error in the Lithuanian Bronze ingestion runbook: the diagnosis command used a hostname changed 3 months earlier, producing a misleading error. The playbook was updated. The third was a missing alert rule: the metadata database latency injection caused scheduling delay but did not trigger an alert because the threshold monitored average latency, not P95. The alert was updated to monitor P95.

blocks.title: Chaos engineering requires organizational maturity
blocks.content: Chaos engineering in a platform without monitoring, alerting, playbooks, and incident response is not chaos engineering — it is just chaos. The prerequisites for chaos testing are: platform-level observability that detects injected failures (lesson 1), alerting that routes incidents to the correct team (lesson 3), playbooks that document the recovery procedure (lesson 5), and a post-mortem process that captures the lessons learned (lesson 6). BalticSolar did not begin chaos testing until Month 6 — after all four prerequisites were operational. Starting earlier would have injected failures that nobody could detect or recover from, damaging confidence in the platform rather than building it.

Capacity-related incidents — worker fleet exhaustion, metadata database connection pool saturation, scheduler heartbeat budget exceeded — are the most preventable category of incidents because they are caused by gradual resource growth that is predictable from historical trends. BalticSolar's capacity monitoring tracks current utilization across four resource dimensions, and the capacity forecasting model projects when each dimension will reach its operational threshold, enabling the platform team to plan infrastructure changes months before the capacity limit is reached rather than reacting to a capacity incident during the midnight batch window.
The Four Capacity Dimensions

The capacity table reveals that connection pool utilization is the nearest constraint at 80% current, with the threshold projected for Q2 2027. This finding drove the platform team to increase the PgBouncer pool size from 15 to 20 connections in the next infrastructure change window — a preventive action that extends the threshold to Q4 2028. The scheduler utilization at 64% has the highest growth rate (+0.1s per quarter, driven by DAG count growth from alliance expansion), and the projected threshold of Q4 2027 aligns with the expected addition of 30+ Estonian regulatory DAGs during the next compliance cycle. The platform team has pre-approved the standalone DAG processor migration (Module 6, lesson 8) as the remediation for when scheduler utilization approaches 80%.
The Forecasting Model

"""BalticSolar capacity forecasting model.

Projects resource utilization trends to identify when each
capacity dimension will reach its operational threshold.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta


@dataclass
class CapacityProjection:
    dimension: str
    current_value: float
    current_capacity: float
    threshold_pct: float              # operational threshold as fraction
    quarterly_growth: float           # absolute growth per quarter
    projected_threshold_date: date | None

    @property
    def utilization_pct(self) -> float:
        return (self.current_value / self.current_capacity) * 100

    @property
    def headroom_pct(self) -> float:
        threshold_value = self.current_capacity * self.threshold_pct
        return ((threshold_value - self.current_value) / self.current_capacity) * 100

    def project_threshold_date(self, from_date: date = None) -> date | None:
        """Calculate when the growth trend will reach the threshold."""
        from_date = from_date or date.today()
        if self.quarterly_growth <= 0:
            return None  # no growth — threshold never reached

        threshold_value = self.current_capacity * self.threshold_pct
        remaining = threshold_value - self.current_value
        if remaining <= 0:
            return from_date  # already at/above threshold

        quarters_to_threshold = remaining / self.quarterly_growth
        days_to_threshold = int(quarters_to_threshold * 91)
        return from_date + timedelta(days=days_to_threshold)


# Lithuanian instance capacity projections — updated quarterly
LT_PROJECTIONS = [
    CapacityProjection(
        dimension='scheduler_heartbeat_sec',
        current_value=3.2,
        current_capacity=5.0,
        threshold_pct=0.80,
        quarterly_growth=0.1,
    ),
    CapacityProjection(
        dimension='worker_peak_concurrent_tasks',
        current_value=28,
        current_capacity=40,
        threshold_pct=0.85,
        quarterly_growth=3,
    ),
    CapacityProjection(
        dimension='db_storage_gb',
        current_value=2.1,
        current_capacity=20,
        threshold_pct=0.70,
        quarterly_growth=0.125,
    ),
    CapacityProjection(
        dimension='pgbouncer_connections',
        current_value=12,
        current_capacity=15,
        threshold_pct=0.90,
        quarterly_growth=0.25,
    ),
]

The forecasting model is deliberately simple — linear extrapolation of quarterly growth rates. BalticSolar chose linear over more sophisticated models (exponential growth, seasonal adjustment) because the uncertainty in the growth rate (will the alliance add 10 or 30 DAGs next quarter?) dwarfs the accuracy difference between linear and exponential projection at the 12–24 month horizon. The model's value is not precise prediction — it is early warning. A projection that says 'connection pool reaches threshold in Q2 2027 plus or minus 2 quarters' is actionable (start planning the upgrade now) even though it is imprecise. The quarterly capacity review updates the growth rates based on actual data, re-projecting the threshold dates and adjusting the infrastructure planning accordingly.
The Quarterly Capacity Review

Every quarter, the platform team updates the capacity projections with actual utilization data and presents the updated forecasts to the alliance infrastructure committee. The review covers four questions: which dimensions are within 2 quarters of their threshold (immediate action required), which dimensions have growth rates that changed significantly from the previous quarter (projections need recalibration), which planned platform changes (new DAGs, new teams, Airflow upgrades) will affect capacity in the next quarter, and which remediation actions from the previous review have been completed. The review produces a prioritized infrastructure change list that feeds into the next quarter's deployment schedule. BalticSolar's infrastructure budget is planned 12 months ahead using the capacity projections, with a 20% contingency buffer for unexpected growth.

blocks.title: Capacity incidents are the most preventable and least excusable
blocks.content: Every other incident category — external dependencies, code bugs, infrastructure outages — involves some element of unpredictability. Capacity incidents are fully predictable: resource consumption trends are visible in the monitoring data, growth rates are calculable from historical data, and threshold dates are projectable from the growth rates. A capacity incident means the platform team saw the trend, could have projected the threshold, and did not act in time. The capacity forecasting model and quarterly review exist to make this failure mode organizationally impossible — the forecast is documented, the threshold is published, and the remediation is planned before the threshold is reached.

The preceding lessons covered what to monitor, how to detect incidents, how to classify severity, and how to route alerts to the correct team. This lesson covers the equally important discipline of not alerting — suppressing, muting, and retiring alerts that generate noise without enabling action. Alert hygiene is the governance practice that keeps the alerting system useful by preventing the alert volume from growing beyond the on-call engineer's capacity to process. BalticSolar's target is fewer than 5 actionable alerts per day across the alliance. Every alert above that threshold is a candidate for elimination, and the quarterly alert hygiene review systematically evaluates every active alert rule against the criterion: does this alert enable a specific action that would not happen without it?
The Alert Hygiene Criteria

rows: The alert has a specific playbook entry that tells the on-call engineer what to do
The alert has no documented response — the engineer must investigate from scratch
Add a playbook entry or delete the alert
The alert fires when human intervention is needed and possible
The alert fires for conditions that resolve automatically (retry succeeds, autoscaling adds capacity)
Convert to a dashboard metric — informational, not alerting
The alert fires < 2× per week on average
The alert fires daily or more frequently, becoming background noise
Increase the threshold, add hysteresis, or aggregate into a weekly summary
The on-call engineer acknowledges and acts on > 80% of firings
The alert is consistently ignored (< 50% acknowledgment rate)
The alert is not useful — eliminate it or escalate its severity
The alert detects a condition that no other alert covers
The alert duplicates another alert's detection with different wording
Deduplicate — keep the more specific alert, retire the redundant one

blocks.content: Alert Suppression During Maintenance

During scheduled maintenance windows (Module 3, lesson 4), all non-P1 alerts are suppressed for the affected instance. The suppression is automated: the maintenance window definition in the scheduling calendar includes an alert_suppression field that the monitoring system reads before evaluating alert rules. P1 alerts (scheduler failure, metadata DB unreachable) are never suppressed because these conditions require immediate response even during maintenance — they indicate that the maintenance itself has caused an unexpected failure. The suppression window extends 30 minutes beyond the maintenance window end to accommodate the startup transient: Airflow instances produce a burst of catch-up scheduling activity after restart that can temporarily trigger scheduling delay alerts.
Alert Muting During Incident Response

When a P1 or P2 incident is active, the alerting system mutes all P3 and P4 alerts for the affected instance. The muting is triggered by the incident declaration in PagerDuty and lasts until the incident is resolved. Without muting, a database latency incident that causes 15 DAGs to fail generates 15 P3 task-failure alerts in addition to the P1 database alert — the 15 P3 alerts are symptoms of the root cause that the platform team is already investigating, and their notifications distract the on-call engineer from the P1 investigation. After the incident is resolved, the muted alerts are evaluated: any P3 alert that was muted because it was a symptom of the resolved incident is automatically cleared, while any P3 alert that persists after the root cause is fixed represents a separate issue that needs attention.
The Quarterly Alert Hygiene Review

Every quarter, the platform team reviews all active alert rules against the hygiene criteria. The review examines three data sources: the alert firing history (how often did each alert fire?), the acknowledgment history (how often was the alert acknowledged within the response time target?), and the incident correlation data (how often was the alert the first signal of a real incident vs noise?). Alerts that fail the hygiene criteria are either improved (threshold adjustment, better deduplication, added playbook entry) or eliminated (removed from the monitoring configuration). BalticSolar's first-year alert hygiene trajectory: Q1 had 23 active alert rules generating 12 alerts/day average; Q4 had 18 active rules generating 3.5 alerts/day. The reduction came from eliminating 8 non-actionable alerts and tightening thresholds on 5 others, while adding 3 new alerts for failure modes discovered by chaos engineering.

blocks.title: An alert that nobody reads is worse than no alert
blocks.content: A non-actionable alert does not just waste the on-call engineer's time — it trains the engineer to ignore alerts. Every ignored alert makes the next alert 5% more likely to be ignored, because the engineer's learned response is 'this is probably another false positive.' The alert fatigue spiral is difficult to reverse once it starts: the engineer needs to see a sustained period of high-quality, actionable alerts before they re-learn to trust the alerting system. BalticSolar's policy — every alert must have a documented response action or it is deleted — prevents the spiral from starting by ensuring that every alert the engineer sees deserves attention.

This lesson synthesizes the entire module into BalticSolar Alliance's observability and incident architecture. Each preceding lesson addressed one dimension: platform-level observability (lesson 1), SLA governance (lesson 2), alerting architecture (lesson 3), incident detection (lesson 4), response playbooks (lesson 5), post-mortem process (lesson 6), chaos engineering (lesson 7), capacity forecasting (lesson 8), and alert hygiene (lesson 9). This lesson traces how these dimensions compose into a complete operational framework and how the framework has evolved through 12 months of production operation across the alliance's four Airflow instances.
The Observability Architecture Summary

rows: P1–P4 severity with correlation engine and team routing
Task failure + duration anomaly + success rate + freshness + cascade
Mean time to detect: 2.3 minutes for P1 incidents
Blameless template with root cause taxonomy and action tracking
3 resilience gaps found and fixed in Year 1
Linear projection on 4 dimensions, quarterly review

blocks.content: The Incident Lifecycle — End to End

The observability architecture's value is best understood through the lifecycle of an incident from detection to prevention of recurrence. A metadata database latency spike occurs on the Lithuanian instance at 01:15 EET. The monitoring system detects P95 query latency exceeding 50ms within 15 seconds (lesson 1). The anomaly detector flags 3 DAGs with duration anomalies within 2 minutes (lesson 4). The correlation engine groups the alerts into a single P1 incident (lesson 3). PagerDuty pages the Lithuanian platform engineer (lesson 3). The engineer opens the playbook for metadata database latency (lesson 5), identifies that a VACUUM overlapped with the midnight batch, and adjusts the maintenance schedule (Module 7, lesson 4). Total time from detection to resolution: 18 minutes. The post-mortem (lesson 6) produces an action item to prevent the VACUUM-batch overlap. The next chaos test (lesson 7) validates the fix.
How Observability Connects to the Full Track

The observability architecture is the operational layer for every architectural decision in Modules 1–8. The platform topology (Module 1) determines the monitoring scope — 4 instances with cross-instance aggregation. The DAG factory (Module 2) enables consistent alerting because every factory-generated DAG has the same SLA parameter structure and the same alerting callback. The scheduling architecture (Module 3) provides the SLA tier framework that determines alert severity. The executor architecture (Module 4) provides the worker fleet metrics that capacity monitoring tracks. The integration architecture (Module 5) provides the integration contract error classifications that the anomaly detector monitors. The scheduler internals (Module 6) provide the heartbeat and parsing metrics that platform health monitoring watches. The metadata database (Module 7) provides the query latency metrics that are often the first signal of a systemic issue. The multi-tenant governance (Module 8) provides the RBAC-based alert routing that ensures alerts reach the correct team.
Looking Forward: Enterprise Governance

Module 10 ties the entire track together with enterprise governance processes that sustain the platform over years. The observability architecture from this module feeds into Module 10's capacity planning (long-term infrastructure budgeting based on capacity projections), disaster recovery (the monitoring system is the detection layer for DR events), and platform lifecycle management (the incident trend analysis reveals when the platform's architecture needs evolution). Module 10 also covers the organizational structures — team models, on-call rotations, governance committees — that operate the observability framework on an ongoing basis.

blocks.title: Observability is the platform's nervous system
blocks.content: The platform architecture (Module 1) is the skeleton. The DAG factory (Module 2) and scheduling architecture (Module 3) are the muscles. The executor (Module 4) and integration layer (Module 5) are the hands that interact with the external world. The scheduler internals (Module 6) and metadata database (Module 7) are the brain. The governance framework (Module 8) is the immune system. And the observability architecture is the nervous system — the sensory network that detects pain, reports it to the brain, and enables the reflexive response that prevents damage from escalating. A platform without observability is a body without nerves: it can function, but it cannot feel when something is wrong until the damage is severe.

chapters.title: Enterprise Governance and Capacity Planning
