chapters.description: Establish the organizational and operational frameworks that sustain a multi-country orchestration platform over years — platform lifecycle management, version upgrade strategy, capacity planning, DAG deprecation, disaster recovery, team structures, and the governance processes that hold the architecture together.

blocks.content: An orchestration platform is not a static artifact — it is a living system that grows, evolves, and eventually requires replacement. BalticSolar's Airflow platform has passed through three distinct lifecycle phases in 18 months: the single-team phase (SaulėGrid's original 3-DAG deployment), the multi-team phase (SaulėGrid scaling to 12 DAGs across 3 teams), and the multi-country phase (BalticSolar Alliance with 200+ DAGs across 10 teams and 3 countries). Each phase transition was triggered by an architectural inflection point — a moment when the existing architecture's capacity was exceeded by the growing workload — and each transition required not just new infrastructure but new governance, new organizational patterns, and new operational practices. Understanding the lifecycle phases and their inflection points enables the platform team to anticipate the next transition rather than being surprised by it.
The Three Lifecycle Phases

headers: Inflection Point That Triggered Next Phase
rows: Single Airflow instance, manual DAG authoring, informal conventions
DAG count exceeded 10 → naming collisions, manual review bottleneck
Single instance with RBAC, CI/CD pipeline, ownership registry
RBAC roles, code review, SLA definitions
Acquisition → 3 countries, data residency requirements, 200+ DAGs
Hybrid topology, DAG factory, scheduling governance, multi-tenant isolation
Next: DAG count approaching 500 → scheduler architectural limit

blocks.content: The Platform Maturity Model

BalticSolar assesses platform health through a maturity model that evaluates six dimensions on a 1–5 scale: automation (1 = manual DAG authoring, 5 = factory-generated with config-driven templates), governance (1 = no RBAC, 5 = full multi-tenant isolation with compliance auditing), observability (1 = Airflow UI only, 5 = cross-instance Prometheus/Grafana with anomaly detection and chaos testing), reliability (1 = no SLAs, 5 = tiered SLAs with 99.9% platinum achievement), scalability (1 = single instance, 5 = federated topology with capacity forecasting), and knowledge (1 = tribal knowledge, 5 = comprehensive ADRs, playbooks, and onboarding documentation). The maturity assessment runs annually and produces a radar chart that highlights dimensions where investment is needed. BalticSolar's current scores: automation 4, governance 4, observability 4, reliability 3.5, scalability 3.5, knowledge 3 — the knowledge dimension is the acknowledged weak point, driving the investment in documentation (lesson 5).
Anticipating the Next Inflection Point

The multi-country phase's architecture was designed for 200–500 DAGs across 3 countries. The capacity forecasting model (Module 9, lesson 8) projects that the Lithuanian instance will approach the scheduler's performance ceiling at approximately 350 DAGs — projected for mid-2028 based on current growth rates. The next inflection point will require one of three architectural responses: the standalone DAG processor (splitting parsing from scheduling, available in Airflow 2.7+), instance sharding within a country (splitting Lithuanian DAGs across two instances based on SLA tier or domain), or migration to a managed Airflow service (Amazon MWAA or Astronomer) that abstracts the scaling challenge. The platform team evaluates these options annually as part of the technology radar process (lesson 8), and the capacity projection determines which option must be implemented and when.

blocks.title: Platform architecture is a sequence of designs, not a single design
blocks.content: The architecture described in Modules 1–9 is not the final architecture — it is the architecture that fits the current scale. When the scale changes (more DAGs, more countries, new regulatory requirements), the architecture must evolve. The lifecycle phases are not failures of the previous architecture — they are the natural consequence of growth exceeding the design's capacity. Senior platform engineers design for the current scale with awareness of the next inflection point, not for an imagined final state that may never arrive. Over-engineering for 1,000 DAGs when you have 200 wastes resources on architectural complexity that may never be needed.

Airflow releases minor versions every 2–3 months and major versions every 12–18 months. Each release includes bug fixes, performance improvements, new features, and occasionally breaking changes that affect DAG code, provider compatibility, or metadata database schema. At BalticSolar scale with 4 instances, 200+ DAGs, a custom provider package, and 2 plugins, upgrading Airflow is not a routine maintenance task — it is a coordinated multi-week operation that requires compatibility assessment, staging validation, canary deployment, and progressive rollout. The upgrade strategy must balance the benefit of new features and security patches against the risk of introducing regressions into a production platform that serves 10 teams across 3 countries with regulatory SLAs.
The Upgrade Assessment Process

rows: Release notes review: identify breaking changes, deprecations, new features, security fixes
Impact assessment document listing affected DAGs, providers, plugins
Yes — upgrade does not proceed without impact assessment
Provider compatibility test: install new Airflow version with balticsolar-airflow-provider in an isolated environment
Provider test suite results — pass/fail with error details for failures
Yes — provider must pass 100% of tests
Staging deployment: deploy new version to shared staging, run full DAG suite
Staging validation report — DAG parsing success rate, task execution results, SLA simulation
Yes — staging must achieve 99% DAG parsing success
Migration rehearsal: run Alembic migrations on staging database with production-volume data (Module 7, lesson 5)
Migration timing, lock analysis, rollback test result
Yes — migration must complete within maintenance window
Canary deployment: upgrade Lithuanian production instance, monitor for 48 hours
Canary report — scheduling delay, SLA achievement, error rate comparison vs pre-upgrade baseline
Yes — canary metrics must not degrade beyond 5% of baseline
Progressive rollout: upgrade Latvia (week 2), Estonia (week 3), coordinator (week 4)
No — each instance can proceed independently after canary success

blocks.content: The total upgrade timeline is 4–6 weeks from release notes review to full rollout. This pace may seem slow for a minor version upgrade, but the cost of a regression that affects 200+ production DAGs across 3 countries — cascading SLA breaches, regulatory filing delays, and 10 teams' confidence in the platform — vastly exceeds the cost of a thorough validation process. BalticSolar's upgrade history demonstrates the value: the Airflow 2.7 to 2.8 upgrade took 5 weeks, and the canary deployment on the Lithuanian instance revealed that the new version changed the Dataset trigger deduplication behavior, which would have caused 15 consumer DAGs to trigger twice per data interval on the other instances if the change had not been caught.
The Rollback Procedure

#!/bin/bash
# BalticSolar Airflow version rollback procedure.
# Reverts an instance to the previous Airflow version.
#
# Prerequisites:
# - Pre-upgrade metadata database backup exists (Module 7, lesson 5)
# - Previous Airflow Docker image is available in the container registry
# - Previous balticsolar-airflow-provider wheel is available

set -euo pipefail

INSTANCE="${1:?Usage: rollback.sh <instance_name> <previous_version>}"
PREVIOUS_VERSION="${2:?}"

echo "=== Rollback ${INSTANCE} to Airflow ${PREVIOUS_VERSION} ==="

# Step 1: Scale down the current deployment
echo "Step 1: Scaling down current deployment..."
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" scale deployment airflow-scheduler --replicas=0
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" scale deployment airflow-webserver --replicas=0
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" scale deployment airflow-worker --replicas=0

# Wait for pods to terminate
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" wait --for=delete pod -l component=scheduler --timeout=120s
echo "All Airflow pods terminated."

# Step 2: Restore the metadata database to pre-upgrade state
echo "Step 2: Restoring metadata database..."
BACKUP_FILE=$(ls -t /backups/airflow/${INSTANCE}/*_pre_upgrade_*.sql | head -1)
echo "Using backup: ${BACKUP_FILE}"

pg_restore     --host="${AIRFLOW_DB_HOST}"     --dbname="${AIRFLOW_DB_NAME}"     --username="${AIRFLOW_DB_USER}"     --clean     --if-exists     "${BACKUP_FILE}"

echo "Database restored."

# Step 3: Update the Helm values to the previous version
echo "Step 3: Reverting Helm deployment..."
helm upgrade airflow-${INSTANCE} apache-airflow/airflow     --namespace "airflow-${INSTANCE}"     --set images.airflow.tag="${PREVIOUS_VERSION}"     --set "env[0].name=AIRFLOW_VERSION,env[0].value=${PREVIOUS_VERSION}"     --reuse-values

# Step 4: Scale back up
echo "Step 4: Scaling up previous version..."
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" scale deployment airflow-scheduler --replicas=2
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" scale deployment airflow-webserver --replicas=1
kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" scale deployment airflow-worker --replicas=8

# Step 5: Verify
echo "Step 5: Verifying rollback..."
sleep 30  # wait for pods to start

CURRENT_VERSION=$(kubectl -n "airflow-${INSTANCE}" exec deploy/airflow-webserver -- airflow version)
echo "Current Airflow version: ${CURRENT_VERSION}"

if [[ "${CURRENT_VERSION}" == *"${PREVIOUS_VERSION}"* ]]; then
    echo "=== Rollback successful: ${INSTANCE} is running ${PREVIOUS_VERSION} ==="
else
    echo "=== WARNING: Version mismatch. Expected ${PREVIOUS_VERSION}, got ${CURRENT_VERSION} ==="
    exit 1
fi

The rollback procedure has a critical dependency: the pre-upgrade metadata database backup (Module 7, lesson 5). Without this backup, the rollback can revert the Airflow code to the previous version but cannot revert the database schema — Alembic migrations that added tables or columns will leave the database in a schema state that the previous Airflow version does not expect. The backup restore step is what makes the rollback complete: code, database schema, and database data all return to their pre-upgrade state. BalticSolar's policy is that no upgrade proceeds without a verified backup that can be restored within the maintenance window. The backup verification (pg_restore --list) runs as part of the pre-upgrade checklist, and the restoration is rehearsed on the staging database before every production upgrade.
Version Pinning and Provider Compatibility

The Airflow version and the balticsolar-airflow-provider version are pinned together in the deployment configuration. A Helm values file specifies both versions, and the CI pipeline validates that the provider's test suite passes against the specified Airflow version before any deployment. This pinning prevents the scenario where an Airflow upgrade silently breaks the provider: the provider is tested against the new Airflow version before the upgrade reaches any instance, and the test results are part of the upgrade assessment. BalticSolar maintains a compatibility matrix that maps each provider version to the Airflow versions it has been tested against, and the deployment pipeline refuses to deploy a provider-Airflow combination that is not in the matrix.

blocks.title: Never upgrade all instances simultaneously
blocks.content: The canary deployment pattern is not optional. BalticSolar's 48-hour canary window has caught 2 issues in 4 upgrades that would have affected all three country instances. The first was the Dataset trigger deduplication change that affected 15 consumer DAGs. The second was a PgBouncer connection pool exhaustion caused by the new version's increased connection usage during scheduler HA coordination. Both issues were resolved on the Lithuanian canary instance before the Latvian and Estonian instances were upgraded. Without the canary, both issues would have caused simultaneous SLA breaches across the alliance.

Module 9 (lesson 8) covered capacity monitoring and short-term forecasting — tracking current utilization and projecting when resource thresholds will be reached. This lesson covers the strategic capacity planning process that translates those forecasts into infrastructure budgets, procurement decisions, and architectural roadmap items. Capacity planning at BalticSolar operates on three time horizons: tactical (next quarter — what infrastructure changes are needed to maintain current headroom?), strategic (next 12 months — what is the annual infrastructure budget based on projected growth?), and architectural (next 2–3 years — when will the current architecture's design limits require a fundamental redesign?). Each horizon feeds different organizational processes: tactical feeds the deployment schedule, strategic feeds the budget request, and architectural feeds the technology roadmap.
The Three Planning Horizons

rows: Current utilization metrics + known upcoming changes (new teams, new DAGs, scheduled migrations)
Infrastructure change requests: pool size adjustments, worker fleet scaling, database hardware upgrades
Q2 2026: increase PgBouncer pool from 15→20 connections on LT instance (projected to hit 90% in Q3)
Capacity forecasting model (Module 9, lesson 8) + alliance growth plan + new country expansion
Annual infrastructure budget: cloud compute, storage, managed services, licensing
FY2027: €285K infrastructure budget covering 4 instances + 22% growth buffer
Technology trend analysis + Airflow roadmap + regulatory landscape changes
Architecture evolution decisions: standalone DAG processor adoption, managed service migration, instance topology changes
2028: evaluate standalone DAG processor when LT scheduler utilization reaches 80%

blocks.content: The Annual Budget Model

"""BalticSolar infrastructure budget model.

Translates capacity projections into annual infrastructure
cost estimates for the alliance finance committee.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class InstanceBudget:
    instance: str
    compute_monthly_eur: float      # Airflow pods (scheduler, web, workers)
    database_monthly_eur: float     # Managed PostgreSQL
    storage_monthly_eur: float      # S3/object storage for logs, XCom, backups
    monitoring_monthly_eur: float   # Prometheus, Grafana, PagerDuty
    vault_monthly_eur: float        # HashiCorp Vault (shared across instances)

    @property
    def monthly_total(self) -> float:
        return (self.compute_monthly_eur + self.database_monthly_eur +
                self.storage_monthly_eur + self.monitoring_monthly_eur +
                self.vault_monthly_eur)

    @property
    def annual_total(self) -> float:
        return self.monthly_total * 12


BALTICSOLAR_BUDGET_FY2027 = [
    InstanceBudget(
        instance='lt',
        compute_monthly_eur=3200,    # 2 schedulers + 1 webserver + 8-15 workers
        database_monthly_eur=450,    # db.r6g.large + read replica
        storage_monthly_eur=180,     # logs, XCom S3 backend, backups
        monitoring_monthly_eur=120,  # share of central Prometheus/Grafana
        vault_monthly_eur=85,        # share of Vault cluster
    ),
    InstanceBudget(
        instance='lv',
        compute_monthly_eur=2400,    # 2 schedulers + 1 webserver + 5 Celery + K8s pods
        database_monthly_eur=280,    # db.r6g.medium
        storage_monthly_eur=120,
        monitoring_monthly_eur=120,
        vault_monthly_eur=85,
    ),
    InstanceBudget(
        instance='ee',
        compute_monthly_eur=2100,    # 2 schedulers + 1 webserver + on-demand K8s pods
        database_monthly_eur=280,    # db.r6g.medium
        storage_monthly_eur=100,
        monitoring_monthly_eur=120,
        vault_monthly_eur=85,
    ),
    InstanceBudget(
        instance='coordinator',
        compute_monthly_eur=800,     # 1 scheduler + 1 webserver + minimal workers
        database_monthly_eur=150,    # db.r6g.small
        storage_monthly_eur=50,
        monitoring_monthly_eur=120,
        vault_monthly_eur=85,
    ),
]

def generate_budget_summary():
    total_monthly = sum(b.monthly_total for b in BALTICSOLAR_BUDGET_FY2027)
    total_annual = sum(b.annual_total for b in BALTICSOLAR_BUDGET_FY2027)
    growth_buffer = total_annual * 0.22  # 22% for growth and contingency

    return {
        'monthly_total_eur': round(total_monthly),
        'annual_base_eur': round(total_annual),
        'growth_buffer_eur': round(growth_buffer),
        'annual_total_eur': round(total_annual + growth_buffer),
        'per_instance': {
            b.instance: {
                'monthly': round(b.monthly_total),
                'annual': round(b.annual_total),
            } for b in BALTICSOLAR_BUDGET_FY2027
        },
    }

The budget model makes infrastructure cost visible and plannable. The monthly breakdown by instance enables the finance committee to understand where the money goes: Lithuania's higher compute cost (€3,200/month vs Estonia's €2,100) reflects its larger worker fleet for 80 DAGs versus 40. The 22% growth buffer covers anticipated DAG count growth (15% based on historical trends) plus a contingency for unplanned infrastructure changes (7%). BalticSolar's cloud cost optimization — achieved by right-sizing database instances, implementing worker fleet autoscaling, and pre-purchasing reserved instances — reduced the FY2027 budget by 22% compared to the Year 1 actual spend, even though the DAG count grew by 35%.
Cost Optimization Strategies

The platform team identifies cost optimization opportunities quarterly as part of the capacity review. The three highest-impact optimizations at BalticSolar were worker fleet autoscaling (reducing the Lithuanian general worker pool from 15 fixed workers to 8–15 autoscaled workers saved €840/month by scaling down during off-peak hours), database right-sizing (the Estonian instance was initially provisioned with db.r6g.large based on the Lithuanian template, but its smaller workload needed only db.r6g.medium — a €170/month savings), and reserved instance purchasing (committing to 1-year reserved instances for the baseline worker count across all instances saved 35% on compute compared to on-demand pricing). Each optimization was evaluated against its risk profile: autoscaling introduces the risk of insufficient capacity during demand spikes, right-sizing reduces headroom, and reserved instances reduce flexibility.

blocks.title: Capacity planning is a conversation between engineering and finance
blocks.content: The platform team understands resource utilization and growth projections. The finance team understands budget constraints and investment priorities. Capacity planning works when both sides contribute: engineering provides the forecasts and cost models, finance provides the budget envelope and prioritization criteria. A capacity plan that engineering produces in isolation will be rejected by finance for lacking business justification. A budget that finance sets without engineering input will be too small for the projected growth or too large for the actual need. BalticSolar's quarterly capacity review includes both teams, and the budget model is a shared artifact that both sides can interrogate.

DAGs have lifecycles: they are created to serve a business requirement, they run in production for months or years, and eventually the requirement changes, the source system is decommissioned, or a factory-generated replacement makes the hand-crafted original redundant. Without a structured deprecation process, obsolete DAGs accumulate on the platform — consuming scheduling capacity, occupying worker slots during retries, cluttering the UI, and creating confusion about which DAGs are authoritative. BalticSolar's deprecation registry tracks 47 DAGs that were deprecated during the first year: 23 were replaced by factory-generated equivalents (Module 2, lesson 8), 12 were decommissioned when their source systems were retired, 8 were consolidated into broader pipelines, and 4 were experimental DAGs that were never promoted to production.
The Deprecation Lifecycle

rows: DAG runs on its production schedule, monitored by SLA framework, maintained by owning team
Owning team files deprecation request with justification. Platform team updates the deprecation registry. Downstream consumers are notified.
Running — deprecation tag added to DAG metadata
DAG continues running. Downstream consumers migrate to the replacement. Usage monitoring tracks whether consumers have transitioned. Owning team answers questions about migration.
Running — deprecation warning displayed in UI
DAG is paused. Downstream consumers that have not migrated receive a P2 alert. The owning team and platform team review any remaining dependencies.
Paused — still visible in UI, can be re-enabled
DAG file is removed from the repository. Connections, variables, and Vault secrets used exclusively by this DAG are decommissioned. Ownership registry entry is archived.
Removed — no longer exists on the platform
Task instance history is retained per the metadata retention policy (Module 7, lesson 4). Audit trail is retained per compliance requirements (Module 8, lesson 6).
Archived — history available in metadata database

blocks.content: The 90-day deprecation period is the most critical phase. During this window, the owning team monitors the deprecation dashboard to track whether downstream consumers have migrated. The dashboard shows the deprecated DAG's Dataset consumers (from the subscription registry, Module 8, lesson 8), the number of external queries against its output (tracked through data access logs), and the count of any manual triggers by non-owning teams. If a downstream consumer has not migrated by day 60, the platform team contacts the consuming team directly with a migration deadline. The 90-day window accommodates teams that have quarterly release cycles — a team that learns about the deprecation mid-cycle has one full cycle to plan and execute the migration.
The Deprecation Registry

# balticsolar-deprecation-registry.yaml
# Tracks all DAGs in deprecation lifecycle.

deprecated_dags:
  - dag_id: lt_de_meters_bronze_v1
    status: hard_sunset              # removed from platform
    deprecation_date: "2025-12-01"
    sunset_date: "2026-03-01"
    reason: "Replaced by factory-generated lt_de_meters_bronze (v3 factory)"
    replacement: lt_de_meters_bronze
    downstream_consumers_migrated: 3
    archived_date: "2026-03-15"

  - dag_id: lv_analytics_adhoc_billing
    status: soft_sunset              # paused, awaiting hard sunset
    deprecation_date: "2026-02-15"
    soft_sunset_date: "2026-05-15"
    reason: "Consolidated into lv_analytics_billing_gold (factory-generated)"
    replacement: lv_analytics_billing_gold
    downstream_consumers_migrated: 1
    remaining_consumers:
      - team: lv-regulatory
        status: migration_in_progress
        expected_completion: "2026-05-30"

  - dag_id: ee_ml_experiment_v2
    status: deprecation_period
    deprecation_date: "2026-03-01"
    reason: "Experiment concluded. Results incorporated into ee_ml_forecast_gold."
    replacement: null                 # no replacement — experiment was one-time
    downstream_consumers_migrated: 0
    remaining_consumers: []           # no downstream consumers

The registry provides the governance structure that prevents the two most common deprecation failures: premature sunset (removing a DAG before consumers have migrated, causing downstream failures) and indefinite deprecation (announcing deprecation but never completing the sunset, leaving obsolete DAGs running indefinitely). The status field drives automated governance: DAGs in 'deprecation_period' display a deprecation warning in the Airflow UI, DAGs in 'soft_sunset' trigger a P2 alert to remaining consumers, and DAGs in 'hard_sunset' are removed from the repository by the CI pipeline. The remaining_consumers field prevents premature sunset: the hard sunset step cannot proceed until all consumers are migrated or the platform team has explicitly approved an exception.

blocks.title: DAG deprecation is dependency management
blocks.content: Deprecating a DAG is not deleting a file — it is managing a dependency change across the teams and systems that depend on the DAG's output. The deprecation process is identical in structure to API deprecation in software engineering: announce the deprecation, provide a migration path, monitor adoption of the replacement, and remove the old version only after all consumers have migrated. The 90-day window and the consumer tracking dashboard are the mechanisms that prevent the most common deprecation failure: removing the old version before all consumers are ready, which is the data platform equivalent of breaking a production API.

BalticSolar's platform maturity assessment (lesson 1) identified knowledge as the weakest dimension — scoring 3 out of 5, behind automation, governance, observability, and reliability. The knowledge gap manifests in three ways: new team members take 2–3 weeks to become productive because the onboarding documentation is incomplete, architecture decisions made 6 months ago have no recorded rationale because the ADR process was not established until Month 8, and cross-team knowledge transfer during team reorganizations loses institutional context because it relies on verbal handoffs rather than documented procedures. This lesson covers the documentation architecture that addresses each gap: Architecture Decision Records for design rationale, platform documentation for operational knowledge, and the knowledge transfer protocol for personnel transitions.
Architecture Decision Records

An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) documents a significant architectural decision with its context, the options considered, the decision made, and the consequences accepted. BalticSolar's ADR repository contains 23 ADRs from the first 12 months, covering decisions from every module in this track: the hybrid topology decision (Module 1), the DAG factory adoption (Module 2), the tier-based priority system (Module 3), the per-country executor selection (Module 4), the hook-operator separation standard (Module 5), the 60-second zombie detection interval (Module 6), the S3 XCom backend (Module 7), the per-country Vault secret engines (Module 8), and the Prometheus-Grafana observability stack (Module 9). Each ADR answers the question that future engineers will ask: 'why did we choose this approach instead of the obvious alternative?'

# ADR-007: S3 XCom Backend Selection
# balticsolar-adrs/007-s3-xcom-backend.yaml

title: "Custom XCom Backend Using S3"
date: "2025-11-15"
status: accepted
authors: ["Marta V., Platform Engineering"]

context: |
  SaulėGrid's original deployment used the default metadata database
  XCom storage. After the alliance merger, the Estonian ML team began
  passing 50-200 MB model artifacts through XCom, causing metadata
  database performance degradation that affected scheduling for all
  80 Lithuanian DAGs (see incident INC-2025-11-08).

  The metadata database is the scheduler's working memory (Module 7,
  lesson 1). Every byte stored in XCom competes with the scheduler's
  task_instance queries for database I/O bandwidth.

decision: |
  Implement a custom XCom backend that stores payloads > 100 KB
  in S3 and stores only a reference key in the metadata database.

  Selected S3 over Redis (considered) because:
  - S3 provides durable storage with no capacity limit
  - Redis would introduce another stateful service to operate
  - S3 cost is negligible at our XCom volume (~500 payloads/day)
  - The 50-200ms S3 fetch latency is acceptable for task execution
    (not on the scheduler's critical path)

consequences:
  positive:
    - Metadata database no longer stores large BLOB payloads
    - Scheduling performance insulated from XCom payload sizes
    - No upper limit on XCom payload size (S3 limit: 5 TB)
  negative:
    - S3 garbage collection required (weekly DAG, Module 7 lesson 3)
    - S3 fetch adds 50-200ms latency vs local database read
    - Debugging XCom values requires S3 access, not just DB access
  neutral:
    - Transparent to DAG authors (factory sets xcom_backend globally)

The ADR format captures not just what was decided but why — the context section explains the problem (ML team's large XCom payloads degrading scheduling), the decision section explains the choice (S3 over Redis, with rationale), and the consequences section honestly states both the benefits and the costs. Future engineers who encounter the S3 XCom backend and wonder 'why not just use Redis?' can read ADR-007 and understand the trade-off analysis without needing to find the original author. BalticSolar requires an ADR for every decision that meets any of these criteria: affects multiple teams, changes the platform topology or configuration, introduces a new technology or service, or costs more than €1,000/year in infrastructure.
The Documentation Completeness Score

BalticSolar measures documentation health through a completeness score that evaluates five documentation categories: ADRs (is every significant decision documented?), playbooks (does every production DAG have a runbook?), onboarding guides (can a new team member self-serve through onboarding?), integration contracts (does every external system have a contract YAML?), and configuration documentation (are all airflow.cfg overrides documented with rationale?). Each category is scored 0–100% based on coverage, and the composite score is the weighted average. The current composite score is 72% — with ADRs at 85% (3 decisions lack ADRs), playbooks at 100% (CI enforces this), onboarding at 60% (the Estonian onboarding guide is incomplete), integration contracts at 80% (2 minor integrations lack contracts), and configuration at 55% (many configuration parameters lack documented rationale). The documentation completeness score is reviewed quarterly, and categories below 70% receive dedicated documentation sprints.

blocks.title: Documentation debt compounds faster than technical debt
blocks.content: A missing ADR from Month 3 cannot be written accurately in Month 12 because the context, alternatives considered, and decision rationale have faded from memory. A playbook that is not updated after a connection name change will mislead the next on-call engineer who follows it. An onboarding guide that assumes a previous tooling setup will confuse every new team member until someone notices and fixes it. BalticSolar's documentation review process — quarterly completeness scoring with dedicated sprints for low-scoring categories — prevents documentation debt from accumulating beyond the point where it can be recovered.

The organizational structure that supports an orchestration platform determines how fast new capabilities are adopted, how effectively incidents are resolved, and how sustainable the platform is over years. BalticSolar has evolved through two team structures in 18 months — from a centralized model (one platform team builds and operates everything) to a hybrid model (a central platform team provides infrastructure and guardrails, while country teams own their DAGs end-to-end). The evolution was driven by a scaling constraint: the 4-person platform team could not simultaneously operate the infrastructure, develop new capabilities, support 10 teams' DAG development, and respond to incidents. The hybrid model distributes the DAG ownership burden to the country teams while the platform team focuses on infrastructure, tooling, and governance.
The Three Team Models

rows: Builds DAGs, operates infrastructure, handles incidents, onboards teams
Submits requirements, reviews output, consumes data
Consistent DAG quality, uniform standards, single point of accountability
Platform team becomes bottleneck. Pipeline teams are disconnected from their own orchestration.
Provides infrastructure and tooling. No involvement in DAG development.
Owns DAGs end-to-end: development, testing, deployment, monitoring, incident response
Maximum team autonomy. Platform team focuses on infrastructure.
Standards diverge across teams. Incident response quality varies. Knowledge silos form.
Owns infrastructure, factory, governance, monitoring, incident escalation. Reviews DAG standards.
Owns DAGs within the factory framework. Writes configs, tests, deploys, monitors own DAGs. First responder for own DAG incidents.
Standards enforced by factory + CI. Teams own their domain. Platform team can focus.
Requires governance overhead (Module 8). Teams must learn the factory. Platform team still handles platform-level incidents.

blocks.content: BalticSolar's hybrid model works because the DAG factory (Module 2) is the enabling mechanism for self-service DAG development. Without the factory, self-service would produce the inconsistency and standards divergence that the centralized model prevents. With the factory, pipeline teams can develop DAGs through configuration rather than code, the factory enforces platform standards automatically, and the CI pipeline validates every change before it reaches any instance. The platform team's role shifts from 'build DAGs for teams' to 'build the tools that enable teams to build their own DAGs safely.' This shift is the organizational equivalent of the factory's technical shift from hand-crafted DAGs to config-driven orchestration.
The Team Sizing Model

BalticSolar's platform team sizing follows the ratio of 1 platform engineer per 50 production DAGs. At 200+ DAGs, the 4-person team is near capacity. The ratio accounts for infrastructure operations (scheduler, executor, database, monitoring — approximately 40% of capacity), tooling development (factory improvements, provider updates, CI pipeline — approximately 30%), governance (quarterly reviews, compliance, onboarding — approximately 20%), and incident response (on-call, post-mortems, chaos engineering — approximately 10%). When the DAG count approaches 300, the team will need a fifth engineer. The ratio is calibrated from BalticSolar's actual time tracking over 12 months — teams with a higher factory adoption rate need less platform support per DAG because the factory handles the cross-cutting concerns that manual DAG management requires.

blocks.title: The platform team's most important product is not the platform — it is the self-service capability
blocks.content: A platform team that builds DAGs for 10 teams creates a dependency: every new DAG, every DAG change, and every DAG incident flows through the platform team. A platform team that builds the factory, the CI pipeline, the monitoring dashboards, and the onboarding documentation creates independence: teams can build, deploy, and operate their own DAGs within the guardrails that the platform provides. The measure of a mature platform team is not how many DAGs they can build — it is how many DAGs teams can build without them.

Disaster recovery for an orchestration platform addresses a different question than disaster recovery for a data warehouse or an application. The data warehouse's DR priority is data preservation — can the data be recovered? The orchestration platform's DR priority is operational continuity — can the platform resume scheduling within the recovery time objective? The data itself (meter readings, telemetry, weather forecasts) lives in S3 and the data warehouse, not in Airflow. Airflow's critical recovery targets are the metadata database (which contains the scheduling state — active DagRuns, task states, connection credentials) and the DAG repository (which contains the pipeline definitions). Losing the metadata database means losing the scheduling state — the platform does not know which DagRuns are active, which tasks have completed, or what the retry count is. Losing the DAG repository means losing the pipeline definitions — the platform has no DAGs to schedule.
Recovery Objectives by Component

rows: PostgreSQL continuous WAL archiving to S3 + daily full snapshot
Point-in-time recovery from WAL archive to the most recent consistent state
Git repository hosted on GitHub with mirrored backup
Clone the repository to the new instance; the scheduler re-parses all DAGs within 2 minutes
Vault Raft snapshots to S3 every hour
Restore Raft snapshot to a new Vault cluster; unseal with recovery keys
Re-deploy Helm chart with the stored values
Prometheus TSDB snapshots to S3 every 4 hours; Grafana dashboards in Git

blocks.content: The RPO and RTO targets reflect the operational reality of each component's criticality. The metadata database's 1-hour RPO means that a disaster could lose up to 1 hour of scheduling state — active DagRuns that were created in the lost hour would need to be re-triggered. The DAG repository's 0 RPO reflects that Git is a distributed version control system with multiple replicas — losing the instance's local copy does not lose any data because the Git server retains the full history. The secrets store's 1-hour RPO is acceptable because credential rotation is infrequent (quarterly), so the most recent hourly snapshot contains the current credentials. The monitoring stack's 4-hour RPO is the least critical because Prometheus metrics are observational, not operational — losing 4 hours of metrics reduces diagnostic visibility but does not affect scheduling.
The Semi-Annual DR Test

BalticSolar tests disaster recovery semi-annually by simulating a full instance loss: the Estonian instance (chosen because it has the smallest workload and the lowest blast radius) is completely destroyed — all pods terminated, the metadata database dropped, the Vault path cleared — and the recovery team follows the DR procedure to rebuild the instance from backups. The test measures three outcomes: did the metadata database restore complete within the 30-minute RTO? Did DAG scheduling resume within the overall 60-minute RTO? Were there any data losses beyond the RPO window? In the first DR test (Month 9), the metadata database restoration took 42 minutes (exceeding the 30-minute target) because the WAL archive in S3 had not been tested with the current database size. The backup configuration was adjusted, and the second DR test (Month 15) achieved a 22-minute restoration.

blocks.title: Untested backups are not backups — they are hopes
blocks.content: BalticSolar's first DR test revealed that the metadata database backup was functioning correctly (daily snapshots were being created and WAL archiving was continuous), but the restoration procedure had never been tested with production-volume data. The restoration script assumed a database size of approximately 500 MB (the staging size) but production was 2.1 GB, and the restoration exceeded the timeout configured in the recovery script. The backup was technically perfect; the restoration procedure was untested and broken. The DR test's value was not proving that the backup worked — it was proving that the restoration worked at production scale.

Building a multi-country orchestration platform on Apache Airflow is a strategic technology decision with a 5–10 year horizon. The platform team must evaluate not just Airflow's current capabilities but its long-term trajectory: the health of the open-source community, the availability of managed service alternatives, the migration cost if Airflow is no longer the right choice, and the emerging orchestration patterns that may require capabilities beyond Airflow's design model. This lesson covers the technology risk assessment framework that BalticSolar uses to evaluate its Airflow investment annually and the criteria that would trigger a migration to an alternative orchestration platform.
The Technology Risk Assessment

rows: Apache Airflow has 700+ contributors, 34K+ GitHub stars, regular releases, active Slack community, multiple corporate sponsors (Astronomer, Google, Amazon)
Monitor contributor count trend and release cadence annually
Three managed options: Amazon MWAA, Google Cloud Composer, Astronomer Cloud. All actively developed.
Evaluate managed options at each upgrade cycle for cost/feature comparison
MWAA: moderate lock-in (AWS-specific networking, IAM). Composer: moderate (GCP-specific). Astronomer: low (can migrate to self-hosted).
Medium for managed, Low for self-hosted
Self-hosting avoids lock-in but increases operational cost. BalticSolar self-hosts.
Airflow 3.x is on the roadmap with potential breaking changes (new scheduler architecture, updated API, deprecated features)
Track Airflow 3.x development. Budget 8–12 weeks for major version migration when released.
Current workload (200+ DAGs, minutes-scale scheduling) is within Airflow's design range. Edge computing and ML model serving push boundaries (Module 6, lesson 9).
Use hybrid architecture (Airflow for batch orchestration, specialized systems for edge/ML) as established in Module 6
No regulatory body mandates Airflow specifically. Compliance requirements (audit, residency) are met through the governance framework, not the tool.
Governance framework (Module 8) is tool-agnostic — the same RBAC, audit, and residency controls apply to any orchestration platform

blocks.content: The Annual Technology Radar Review

BalticSolar evaluates its orchestration technology stack annually through a technology radar process adapted from ThoughtWorks' model. The radar categorizes technologies into four rings: adopt (use in production), trial (experiment in non-critical workloads), assess (research and evaluate), and hold (do not invest further). Airflow is in the 'adopt' ring. Prefect and Dagster are in the 'assess' ring — the platform team tracks their development and evaluates them at each annual review. Temporal is in the 'assess' ring for event-driven orchestration workloads that exceed Airflow's design parameters. Apache Kafka is in the 'adopt' ring for real-time event processing that complements Airflow's batch orchestration. The radar is reviewed annually and updated when the technology landscape changes — a new orchestration tool that solves a specific BalticSolar pain point moves to the 'trial' ring for hands-on evaluation.
The Migration Exit Criteria

BalticSolar has defined five criteria that would trigger serious evaluation of migrating away from Airflow. First, Airflow's open-source community declines significantly (contributor count drops 50% year-over-year, release cadence slows to fewer than 2 releases per year). Second, a regulatory change requires capabilities that Airflow's architecture cannot support (such as sub-second scheduling for real-time compliance monitoring). Third, the managed Airflow alternatives (MWAA, Composer) discontinue support while the self-hosted operational burden exceeds the platform team's capacity. Fourth, the Airflow 3.x migration introduces breaking changes that require rewriting more than 30% of the DAG factory, provider package, and plugin codebase. Fifth, an alternative orchestration tool achieves feature parity with Airflow while solving one of Airflow's design limitations (sub-second scheduling, native multi-instance coordination, or per-task resource isolation without Kubernetes). None of these criteria are currently met, and the annual review confirms this assessment.

blocks.title: The exit strategy is the most important strategy you never use
blocks.content: Having a documented exit strategy for Airflow is not pessimism — it is risk management. BalticSolar's governance framework (Module 8), DAG factory (Module 2), and integration contracts (Module 5) are all designed to be tool-agnostic at the governance layer: RBAC policies, SLA tiers, and integration contracts describe what the platform does, not how Airflow implements it. If a migration were necessary, the governance framework would transfer to the new platform while the implementation layer is rebuilt. This architecture-governance separation means that a migration changes the implementation, not the organization.

sections.title: What Comes Next — The Staff Engineer

blocks.content: The senior track has covered orchestration platform architecture from deployment topology through scheduler internals through enterprise governance. The knowledge in Modules 1–10 equips the student to design, build, and govern an orchestration platform for a multi-team, multi-country organization. The staff engineer level — beyond this track — extends that capability into three new dimensions: contributing to the orchestration ecosystem rather than just consuming it, designing orchestration patterns for emerging workload types that Airflow was not originally designed for, and leading the organizational transformation that turns a data engineering team into a platform engineering organization. This lesson previews these dimensions and maps each to the senior-level foundation it builds on.
Contributing to the Ecosystem

A senior engineer designs platforms that use Airflow. A staff engineer shapes how Airflow evolves. Contributing to the Airflow open-source project — proposing Airflow Improvement Proposals (AIPs), implementing scheduler performance improvements, adding new executor capabilities, or building community providers — creates leverage that extends beyond the engineer's organization. BalticSolar's platform team has contributed two improvements to the Airflow codebase: a fix for the Dataset trigger deduplication issue discovered during the 2.8 canary deployment (Module 10, lesson 2), and a performance optimization for the task_instance query that the scheduling delay incident (Module 6, lesson 1) revealed as a bottleneck. Each contribution improved Airflow for every user, not just BalticSolar.
Emerging Orchestration Patterns

rows: Airflow triggers Spark/Ray jobs; ML frameworks handle training internally
Native ML pipeline orchestration with experiment tracking, model registry, and A/B deployment integrated into the DAG lifecycle
Kafka handles event streams; Airflow orchestrates batch aggregation of results
Hybrid orchestration where streaming and batch DAGs share scheduling governance, SLA frameworks, and monitoring infrastructure
Single cloud provider (AWS) with per-country regions
Cross-cloud DAG execution where tasks run on different cloud providers based on data locality and cost optimization
New scheduler architecture, updated API surface, potential breaking changes to the DAG authoring model

blocks.content: From Platform Engineer to Organization Leader

The most significant staff-level shift is organizational, not technical. A senior engineer designs a platform. A staff engineer designs the organization that operates the platform. The team structure decisions (lesson 6), the governance committee charter (Module 8, lesson 10), the on-call rotation design (Module 9), and the capacity planning process (lesson 3) are all organizational design decisions that determine whether the platform's technical architecture is sustainable over years. A platform with excellent architecture but poor organizational support — understaffed platform team, missing governance processes, inadequate documentation — will decay as the team that built it rotates and the institutional knowledge dissipates. A platform with adequate architecture but excellent organizational support will evolve and improve because the processes capture decisions, transfer knowledge, and enable continuous adaptation.

The staff engineer's role is to ensure that both the technical architecture and the organizational architecture are healthy. The maturity model from lesson 1 provides the assessment framework. The ADR process from lesson 5 preserves decision rationale. The governance committee from Module 8 maintains compliance and coordination. The quarterly reviews from Modules 3, 5, 8, and 9 provide the feedback loops that detect degradation. Together, these processes create an organization that can operate the platform without depending on any single engineer's presence — which is the ultimate test of a staff engineer's contribution.

blocks.title: The staff engineer's legacy is not the platform — it is the organization's ability to evolve the platform after they leave
blocks.content: Every technical contribution in this track — the factory, the scheduling governance, the observability stack, the governance framework — is a durable artifact that will outlast the engineer who built it. But the ADRs that explain why each decision was made, the onboarding documentation that enables new team members to become productive, the governance processes that maintain the architecture's integrity, and the team structure that distributes ownership sustainably — these organizational contributions are what make the platform resilient to the inevitable personnel changes that every organization experiences. A platform that collapses when its architect leaves was a single point of failure. A platform that evolves after its architect leaves was a well-designed organization.

sections.title: From Platform Engineer to Platform Architect

blocks.content: This is the final lesson of the Apache Airflow Senior-Level Track. Over 100 lessons and 66.7 hours, the track has traced a complete journey: from SaulėGrid's 12-DAG orchestration platform to BalticSolar Alliance's 200+ DAG, 10-team, 3-country enterprise architecture. This lesson synthesizes the entire track by tracing the BalticSolar scenario one final time through every module's core contribution, connecting the pieces into the complete picture of what it means to design, build, and govern an orchestration platform at enterprise scale — the platform architect's holistic view that no single module can provide on its own.
The Track in Ten Modules

rows: Designed the multi-instance topology, namespace conventions, and environment strategy that provide the structural foundation for 200+ DAGs across 3 countries
4 Airflow instances serving 10 teams with physical regulatory isolation
Replaced hand-crafted DAGs with config-driven generation, reducing maintenance from O(n) to O(1) for cross-cutting changes
Factory with template hierarchy and schema validation
Designed the priority, pooling, and scheduling governance that prevent 200+ DAGs from starving each other for resources
Tier-based priority matrix with dedicated platinum/gold pools
Midnight thundering herd eliminated; platinum SLA at 99.7%
Matched executor selection to workload patterns across 3 country instances
Task success rate > 99% across all instances
Designed the integration surface, API contracts, and provider governance for 15+ external systems
Hook-operator separation standard with build-vs-extend framework
7 custom integrations + 8 community providers, 2 retired in Year 1
Read the scheduler source code to diagnose problems that configuration cannot explain
Source-code-level diagnosis of the scheduling delay incident
Composite database index fix reduced scheduling delay from 45s to 5s
Designed the database architecture, XCom backend, maintenance automation, and scaling strategy
S3 XCom backend + PgBouncer + read replica for LT instance
Database performance insulated from XCom payloads and UI query load
Designed the RBAC, data residency isolation, and compliance framework for 3 regulatory regimes
Zero residency violations in 12 months; quarterly compliance audits passing
Designed the monitoring, alerting, incident response, and chaos engineering infrastructure
Prometheus-Grafana-PagerDuty stack with alert correlation engine
Mean time to detect P1: 2.3 min; 3 resilience gaps found by chaos tests
Established the lifecycle, capacity planning, documentation, and organizational structures for long-term sustainability
Hybrid team model + annual technology radar + semi-annual DR test

blocks.content: The Architecture as a Whole

blocks.caption: The complete BalticSolar orchestration platform architecture. Each layer depends on the layers below it: enterprise governance depends on observability, which depends on multi-tenant governance, which depends on the scheduler and database internals, which depend on the integration and executor layers, which depend on the scheduling architecture, which depends on the DAG factory, which depends on the platform architecture foundation.

visual-recommendation.description: Full-stack architecture diagram showing all 10 modules as layers in the BalticSolar orchestration platform, with dependency arrows between layers and key components annotated within each layer. This is the capstone diagram for the entire senior track.

blocks.content: The Senior-Level Promise

The mid-level track's closing lesson (AFI10-10) ended with a promise: the student can schedule, design, test, deploy, secure, tune, and operate a production Airflow platform. The senior track fulfills a deeper promise: the student can design the platform architecture that makes orchestration reliable for everyone. 'Everyone' is the key word — the senior engineer does not build DAGs for 10 teams; they build the factory that enables 10 teams to build their own DAGs safely. They do not tune the scheduler for their own pipeline; they design the scheduling governance that allocates resources fairly across 200+ DAGs. They do not configure RBAC for their team; they design the multi-tenant isolation model that satisfies three regulators simultaneously. The shift from 'I can operate a platform' to 'I can design the platform that makes operations possible for everyone' is the senior-level contribution.

BalticSolar Alliance's orchestration platform is not a finished product — it is a living system that will evolve through new countries joining the alliance, new regulatory requirements emerging, new workload types (ML pipelines, real-time processing) demanding capabilities beyond the current architecture, and Airflow itself evolving through version 3.x and beyond. The governance processes established in this track — the quarterly reviews, the ADR repository, the capacity forecasting, the deprecation lifecycle, the technology radar — ensure that the platform evolves deliberately rather than accidentally. The architecture will change. The governance that guides the evolution will endure.

blocks.title: The orchestration platform architect
blocks.content: You started this track knowing how to build and operate production DAGs. You finish it knowing how to design the platform that makes DAG development, deployment, and operations reliable for 10 teams across 3 countries. The platform architecture from Module 1, the factory from Module 2, the scheduling governance from Module 3, the executor selection from Module 4, the integration contracts from Module 5, the scheduler expertise from Module 6, the database architecture from Module 7, the multi-tenant governance from Module 8, the observability infrastructure from Module 9, and the enterprise governance from this module — together, they form a complete orchestration platform architecture that you can adapt to any organization's scale, regulatory environment, and team structure. You are the platform architect that BalticSolar needed 18 months ago. Go build the platform your organization needs.
